title: Prompt Engineering in Fine-Tuning BiomedCLIP to Enhance COVID-19 Chest X-Ray Diagnosis
authors: Aochen He
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: Large-scale pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs), such as BiomedCLIP, have demonstrated promising potential for biological image classification. However, effectively adapting these large pre-trained VLMs to specialized diagnostic tasks remains challenging due to the scarcity of annotated datasets and the prohibitive computational costs associated with full-model fine-tuning. Prompt engineering, which aims to rapidly adapt pre-trained models on small-scale, task-specific datasets, has emerged as a widely adopted alternative. Nevertheless, current prompt engineering methodologies lack standardization. To address this limitation, this work proposes a novel diagnostic axis framework. It standardizes prompts to systematically capture clinically relevant features while accounting for model sensitivity to stylistic prompt variations. This approach enhances both task adaptation and prompt interpretability for downstream diagnostic applications. On the COVID-19 Radiography Database, the model fine-tuned under the proposed framework achieved a 20.33% improvement in Accuracy compared to the baseline model. Experimental results demonstrate that models fine-tuned using diagnostic axes significantly outperformed zero-shot baseline models across most evaluation metrics on a COVID-19 screening task. Despite these promising results, this study has several limitations, such as insufficient exploration of interactions among different prompt styles and limited support for complex reasoning tasks. Future research could further incorporate potential interactions and extend support for multi-step reasoning to improve the generalizability and practical utility of the method.

title: A Low-Complexity Closed-Form Scheme for Power Minimization in SWIPT Multi-Hop Relay Networks
authors: Yang Yu, Yawen Wang, Benjun Liu, Shuang Hu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a multi-hop decode-and-forward (DF) relay network employing simultaneous wireless information and power transfer (SWIPT), where relay nodes operate in a passive state without relying on batteries. Focusing on green communications, we jointly optimize the power splitting (PS) ratio and the source transmit power to minimize the source power consumption while satisfying the system's minimum quality of service (QoS) requirement. The problem is formulated as a convex optimization problem, and a closed-form optimal solution is derived based on the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions. Simulations demonstrate that the proposed scheme not only achieves optimal performance but also executes with high speed, thereby proving its feasibility for deployment in practical communication scenarios.

title: Research on Personalized Recommendation Method for Web Front End Teaching Courses Based on Implicit Feedback
authors: Lei Ma
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Traditional teaching course recommendation methods mainly rely on user satisfaction or utility evaluation of courses. In web front-end teaching course recommendation, the personalized learning needs of users are ignored, resulting in poor quality of recommended courses. Therefore, to solve the problem of personalized course recommendation, this paper proposes a personalized course recommendation method based on implicit feedback. This method is based on user interaction behavior analysis, implicit feedback data is collected, including multidimensional data such as browsing history, click behavior, and dwell time, and stored in a relational database. Extract user learning features and group user browsing records to discover user groups with similar browsing behaviors. Build a knowledge graph for web-based teaching courses, combined with user interest models, integrate course knowledge points, associations, etc. into recommendation methods, generate personalized recommendation lists, and this approach effectively enhances recommendation accuracy and diversity. To verify the performance of the proposed recommendation method, a control group was set up to conduct comparative analysis based on indicators such as recommendation novelty, recommendation coverage, and normalized cumulative loss gain (NDCG). Experimental results demonstrate the novelty and coverage of recommendations are much higher than traditional methods, and it can cover a wider range of courses. Meanwhile, as the length of the recommendation list increases, the NDCG value maintains a stable growth, proving that this method can more accurately capture user interests and generate high-quality personalized recommendation lists. In future research, the sample size will be expanded to more comprehensively validate the effectiveness of recommendation methods in different user groups.

title: Boundary Recognition System for Intelligent Lawn Mowing Robot Based on mmWave Radar
authors: Aiqing Wang, Yu Qin, Zhiguo Shi, Yong Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: To address the critical challenges of low boundary recognition accuracy, high hardware costs, and computational complexity in wire-free intelligent lawn mowing robot operating in complex environments, this paper proposes a novel boundary detection solution utilizing cost-effective 60 GHz mmWave radar. The system implements a multipath suppression strategy through non-coherent accumulation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4}$</tex>-channel receiver signals, effectively suppressing noise in the acquired radar data. By fusing multidimensional features, the proposed method significantly enhances the distinguishability between grass, water surfaces, and various types of hard pavements. A lightweight random forest classifier was developed, achieving 97.7% material recognition accuracy on the training set and 94.0% on the test set for five material types under various environmental conditions. This work demonstrates the engineering feasibility of combining millimeter-wave radar with machine learning for boundary detection, offering advantages in cost-efficiency, high precision, and computational lightness. The proposed system provides crucial technical support for the evolution of smart lawn mowers from physically restricted operation to fully autonomous navigation.

title: The Factors Influencing Vietnamese University Students' Adoption of Coursera MOOCs: An Exploratory Study Using the UTAUT2 Framework
authors: Ngan Kim Pham, Vi Loi Truong, Nghia Huynh Dai Le
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: This study explores the factors influencing Vietnamese university students' adoption of Coursera MOOCs through the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology 2 (UTAUT2) framework. Data were collected from 229 students at two Vietnamese universities via an online survey and analyzed using SPSS. The multiple linear regression results show that Social Influence <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\boldsymbol{\beta}=0.259, \mathrm{p}<0.001)$</tex>, Facilitating Conditions <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\boldsymbol{\beta}=0.177, \mathrm{p}=0.015)$</tex>, and Hedonic Motivation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\beta=0.284, \mathrm{p}<0.001)$</tex> significantly impact students' Behavioral Intention to adopt MOOCs. In contrast, Performance Expectancy and Habit show no significant effect. All variance inflation factor (VIF) values are below 2, indicating no serious multicollinearity issues. Cronbach's Alpha values exceeded 0.9, confirming strong internal consistency across constructs. These findings underscore the importance of social encouragement, infrastructure support, and enjoyment in promoting MOOC adoption. The study offers practical insights for educators and policymakers to design more engaging and accessible online learning experiences that align with Vietnam's digital transformation goals.

title: A Study on Protection Against Indirect Lightning Effects for Airborne High-Speed Signals
authors: Jiaming Pan, Yeghao Weng, Qi Wang, Yaogen Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: As technology advances, the signal transmission rate of airborne equipment increases, making high-speed signal transmission susceptible to indirect lightning effects. Indirect lightning Effects can generate instantaneous pulse voltages and currents, disrupting signal transmission and potentially damaging equipment. The impact of indirect lightning on airborne high-speed equipment mainly occurs through electromagnetic pulses infiltrating the system via complex electromagnetic coupling paths, causing hardware damage and functional anomalies. This study focuses on indirect lightning protection for airborne high-speed LVDS signals. It combines GDT, TVS, and TBU devices in the design. In high-speed differential PCB layouts, the 3 W spacing rule is strictly followed to minimize crosstalk. Wiring uses a combination of flexible and FR4 high-speed PCB designs to reduce signal transmission impact. Experiments in this study comply with DO-160G waveform 3 and 4, level 3 and 5 and meet the GB/T20240.5-2012 standard for civil aircraft lightning protection design. The findings offer valuable references for protecting airborne high-speed signals from indirect lightning effects.

title: A Lightweight Object Detection Algorithm for Complex Traffic Environments
authors: Yi Wang, Nian Jia, Zengxi Huang, Ziping Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Robust vehicle detection is paramount for urban traffic management in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). However, practical deployment faces significant challenges: dense vehicle clusters, diverse illumination, and the need for lightweight algorithms on embedded systems. While advanced detectors like the YOLO series have improved inference speed and accuracy, balancing high detection performance with stringent model lightweighting in complex traffic remains a critical problem. This paper introduces SSL-YOLO, a novel lightweight object detection model designed for challenging traffic scenarios. Our approach integrates StarNet as the backbone, leveraging its highdimensional feature mapping to enhance feature extraction without increasing computational complexity. The C3K2_Star module further optimizes feature localization and reduces overhead. To address occlusion, a Spatial Enhancement Attention Module (SEAM) is embedded within the detection head, mitigating response loss via spatial and multi-scale feature fusion. On the UA-DETRAC dataset, SSL-YOLO demonstrates superior performance: 30 % fewer parameters than YOLOv11, while simultaneously boosting mAP@0.5 by 5.2% and mAP@0.5:0.95 by 3.5 %. This validates SSL-YOLO's effectiveness in balancing detection accuracy and model lightweighting for real-world applications.

title: An Arithmetic Optimization Algorithm Incorporating Velocity Vectors and Elite Search
authors: Kaibin Zhang, Xueyu Huang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: To address the limitations of the basic arithmetic optimization algorithm, this paper presents a novel arithmetic optimization algorithm (VEAOA) that combines the velocity vector strategy and the elite search strategy. Firstly, the mathematical optimizer acceleration function (MOA) is reconstructed, enabling the algorithm to focus on global search in the early stage and local development in the later stage. Secondly, the velocity increment of the particle swarm algorithm is introduced into the candidate solution update process, which helps maintain the diversity of candidate solutions during iteration, avoids the algorithm from falling into local optima, and improves its convergence accuracy. Finally, an elite search strategy and a parallel execution strategy for candidate solution updates are proposed, preventing underutilization of candidate solutions caused by stagnation in updating the global optimal solution and enhance the algorithm's ability to escape local optima. Through tests on 14 benchmark functions and a typical engineering application, the experimental results demonstrate that the improved arithmetic optimization algorithm achieves faster convergence speed and higher convergence accuracy, and also performs better in practical applications.

title: Co-incineration of RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel) with Wood chips in the Moving Grate Furnace
authors: Dmitrijs Rusovs, Aleksandrs Sorocins, Sandis Gromovs
publish: 2025 IEEE 66th International Scientific Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering of Riga Technical University (RTUCON)
abstract: Waste incineration with energy recovery, commonly known as waste-to-energy (WtE) technology, has become a cornerstone of modern waste management systems. As of 2020, there were 402 WtE plants operating across the EU-27, collectively treating $\mathbf{8 0. 9}$ million tons of waste. However, the direct combustion of municipal solid waste (MSW) poses several challenges, including its low calorific value, high moisture content, and the generation of large amounts of fly ash. These factors can lead to issues such as corrosion and slagging in boilers and flue gas ducts. Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF), which is produced from MSW through shredding, segregation, and screening, offers improved and more uniform chemical and physical properties, as well as a higher calorific value. This enhances the combustion process compared to raw MSW. Furthermore, co-incinerating RDF with conventional fuels can improve boiler efficiency and reduce the burden on flue gas treatment systems. This paper presents the results of RDF co-combustion with wood chips in a moving grate furnace. Due to the complex chemical composition of flue gases from waste combustion, WtE plants are subject to strict emission regulations. The paper discusses the conversion of a 4 MW heatonly biomass grate boiler into a waste co-incineration facility. Key plant modifications include enhanced mixed-fuel preparation, the addition of a post-combustion chamber, and an upgraded flue gas cleaning system in accordance with EU pollution control standards

title: Interpretable Teacher Competence Evaluation via Neural Networks with Inverse Inference
authors: Shifeng Li, Jinyu Wei, Guolong Li, Shuai Guo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: This paper proposes a unified cascaded neural network framework for interpretable and adaptive teacher competence evaluation. Traditional methods often rely on manually defined indicators and lack the ability to provide actionable feedback for performance improvement. To address this limitation, the framework integrates deep learning with inverse inference, enabling the derivation of optimal input feature combinations corresponding to target performance outcomes. A multi-layer perceptron (MLP) model is trained to map teacher-related features to predicted performance scores, and through backward reasoning, it infers feature vectors that represent feasible developmental pathways. Feature importance analysis based on the model's first-layer weights provides meaningful insight into the internal decision-making mechanism, identifying key factors that influence performance predictions. Experimental results on a dataset of 312 full-time teachers demonstrate the model's effectiveness, achieving a test accuracy of 87.3% and an AUC-ROC score of 84.1%. This work offers a data-driven, interpretable approach to teacher evaluation and development planning, with potential applications in broader professional assessment contexts.

title: Descriptor: Appraising Trust in Interactions between Human Supervisors and Autonomous Robotic Systems (ATHAI)
authors: DANIEL A. WILLIAMS, AIRLIE CHAPMAN, DANIEL R. LITTLE, CHRIS MANZIE
abstract: With the ongoing development of autonomous systems, significant attention has focused on deploying autonomous robotic systems to complete a task under human supervision. An important pillar of this interaction is a notion of human trust in the autonomous system, however the modeling of trust in such scenarios has been hampered by the lack of a standard open-access dataset with longitudinal measurements of trust. By recording measurements of trust at a single point in time (often at the end of interactions), these datasets do not capture the temporal dynamics of trust over the course of interactions. We propose to address these challenges with a novel multi-modal dataset that aggregates autonomous system status and task performance, self-reported human trust, and human interventions in the autonomous system during interactions. We collect the data using a software-based interface that simulates a swarm of autonomous robotic agents performing a foraging task in a rectangular area. By recording metrics pertaining to the autonomous robotic swarm’s task performance and human trust, the dataset provides a foundation for the modeling of human trust dynamics and their influence on human interventions. In addition, the dataset’s inclusion of timestamped human responses to a secondary task permits analysis of human attention allocation between tasks over time. It is anticipated that the dataset will provide a benchmark for human trust models, facilitating the development of more trust-aware autonomous robotic systems. IEEE SOCIETY/COUNCIL Robotics and Automation Society (RAS).

title: WECON 2025 TOC
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 8th World Engineering Conference on Contemporary Technologies (WECON)

title: Efficient Malicious Multiparty Private Set Intersection Supporting Cardinality, Sharing, and Batching
authors: Xiyuan Han, Jun Zhou, Zhenfu Cao, Xiaolei Dong, Kim-Kwang Raymond Choo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Efficient and maliciously secure multiparty private set intersection (mPSI)—especially for variants enabling cardinality (mPSI-CA) or secret shared outputs with low communication overhead—faces ongoing challenges regarding performance and scalability. However, existing approaches are either based on strong security assumptions of non-colluding centers or are relatively expensive in terms of computational and communication overheads. Addressing these limitations, this paper introduces a new suite of protocols and formalizes them in a real/ideal model to prove the security of the protocols. Firstly, our core mPSI protocol achieves malicious security under the standard honest majority model, relying solely on symmetric-key primitives. The approach employs a lightweight, oblivious key-value store (OKVS)-based architecture where each non-pivot party sends only a single message to a designated pivot. This approach, inspired by Nevo et al. (CCS 2021), minimizes client overhead by carefully delegating core computations. We extend this framework to support cardinality (mPSI-CA) and secret sharing (mPSI-SS) functionalities, which require an additional non-collusion assumption among specific parties. We also introduce a Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)-based batching technique for parallel mPSI, achieving near-linear communication savings by compressing multiple OKVS structures. This method generally trades higher computational costs (from polynomial operations and CRT) for communication efficiency, but it is highly effective when communication is paramount or when batching numerous instances of small item sets, where encoding computations can be competitive. Finally, our implementation and evaluation of the proposed mPSI and mPSI-CA protocols in both LAN and WAN settings demonstrate their practical advantages. For instance, in the LAN setting depicted (15 parties, t = 7,m = 220), our mPSI protocol is 3.0× faster and uses 2.5× less communication than Nevo et al. (CCS 2021). Against the approach of Gao et al. (CCS 2024), our maliciously secure protocol is 1.4× faster with comparable communication overhead under weaker assumptions.

title: The MIMO-ME-MS Channel: Analysis and Algorithm for Secure MIMO Integrated Sensing and Communications
authors: Seongkyu Jung, Namyoon Lee, Jeonghun Park
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: This paper addresses precoder design for secure multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) systems. We introduce a MIMO channel with a multiple-antenna eavesdropper and a multiple-antenna sensing receiver (MIMO-ME-MS) and analyze the fundamental performance limits of this tripartite tradeoff. Using sensing mutual information, we formulate the precoder design as a nonconvex weighted sum rate maximization problem. A high signal-to-noise ratio analysis based on a subspace decomposition characterizes the maximum weighted degrees of freedom. This analysis reveals the structure of a quasi-optimal precoder that must span the “useful subspace” and demonstrates the inadequacy of extending known schemes from simpler wiretap or ISAC channels. To solve this nonconvex problem, we develop a practical two-stage iterative algorithm that alternates between a sequential basis construction stage and a power allocation stage that solves the resulting difference-of-convex program. We demonstrate that the proposed method captures the desirable precoder structure identified in our analysis and achieves substantial performance gains in the MIMO-ME-MS channel.

title: A Compact Integrated Dual-Port Dual-Mode Device for Independent and Simultaneous Microwave Sensing and Heating
authors: Yan Zheng, Guy A. E. Vandenbosch, Bart Nauwelaers, Tomislav Markovic
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: In this study, a dual-functional microwave microfluidic system is proposed to achieve integrated dielectric sensing and localized heating. Distinct resonance modes supported by the composite right-handed (RH)/left-handed (LH) transmission line structure provide high sensitivity sensing and rapid uniform heating, while orthogonal feeding configurations ensure high functional isolation and stable simultaneous operation. Experiments using ethanol–water mixtures from 0% to 100% demonstrate a high sensitivity of 0.82% for pure water and an average sensitivity of 1.5% across all tested mixtures. At an input power of 1.29 W, the heating port produces localized temperature rise of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$29~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$38~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$44~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C for water and 30% and 60% ethanol–water mixtures, respectively. Simultaneous operation is further validated by tracking the temperature-dependent permittivity of water under microwave heating. These results demonstrate that the proposed device provides an effective and experimentally validated solution for integrated microwave sensing and heating, offering a new microwave platform for studies and applications that require controlled thermal conditions together with real-time dielectric monitoring.

title: Dataflow-Reconfigurable CNN Accelerator Design
authors: Alperen Kalay, Ozcan Ozturk
abstract: Dataflow-level reconfigurability plays a crucial role in Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) acceleration by enabling the selection of the most suitable dataflow pattern for convolution operations. Fully reconfigurable architectures support multiple dataflows and provide high flexibility, but often incur increased design complexity and operational overhead. In contrast, non-reconfigurable architectures optimized for a single dataflow achieve high efficiency for specific workloads but lack adaptability. This paper presents an intermediate dataflow-level reconfigurable CNN accelerator that balances flexibility and efficiency by supporting a limited set of key dataflow patterns. The proposed accelerator identifies the most appropriate dataflow with an average of 0.15% excess latency. Using a specialized systolic array architecture, the design achieves an average of 35% performance improvement compared to non-reconfigurable, single-dataflow architectures.

title: Effects of Mechanical Perturbation Magnitude on Human Gait Entrainment
authors: Omik M. Save, Sidhant Das, Evan Carlson, Anna Kruse, Jooeun Ahn, Hyunglae Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Gait entrainment is a relatively new robotaided rehabilitation approach, and its underlying mechanisms remain underexplored. Investigating how different periodic perturbation parameters influence entrainment characteristics could help bridge this knowledge gap and improve gait rehabilitation protocol designs. This study examines the effect of varying magnitudes of periodic mechanical perturbations on gait entrainment characteristics in lower extremity joints during walking, such as success rate, phase variability, and onset latency. Two distinct soft robotic devices were utilized to perturb the ankle and hip joints, with perturbation magnitudes controlled by adjusting the actuator pressure. Fifteen healthy participants performed walking tasks in separate studies for each joint, with each device perturbing the respective joint at predetermined magnitudes. In the ankle study, a perturbation magnitude corresponding to 3.4% of the peak ankle torque achieved a consistently high entrainment success rate (75.6%). Similarly, in the hip study, a perturbation magnitude equivalent to 7.8% of the peak hip torque yielded a high entrainment success rate (80.0%). Both studies exhibited plateauing trends in entrainment success rate, phase variability, and onset latency, indicating that increases beyond their respective critical magnitude thresholds did not lead to further improvements. These results may be attributed to the recruitment of somatosensory feedback networks as well as mechanisms for optimizing mechanical assistance, which are not necessarily mutually exclusive. Identifying these magnitude thresholds provides a foundation for developing personalized rehabilitation protocols aimed at enhancing neuromotor learning through consistent gait entrainment.

title: Incorporating 3-D-Printed Polymer Scaffolds Onto MEMS Resonant Chemical Sensors for Enhanced Performance
authors: Biya D. Haile, Nikolas T. Roeske, Hongyu Guo, Omer T. Inan, Luke A. Beardslee
abstract: Embedded sensing of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) can enable air monitoring for toxins, healthcare diagnostics and restoration of lost sense of smell. While lab-based instruments for VOC sensing provide sufficient sensitivity and selectivity for these applications, they require samples to be taken and transported for post-hoc analysis and thus real-time monitoring in the field is not possible. A key limitation for portable VOC sensing systems is the lack of scalable and robust manufacturing processes available for the chemical sensor itself. This work presents a two-photon lithography technique for producing sensing films used in chemical sensors for VOCs. The 3D-printed polymer improves both sensor sensitivity and response time compared to spray coated polymers or other film coating techniques. A thick polymer sensing layer applied to the microfabricated sensor’s surface enhances the sensor’s sensitivity. The sensing films were printed directly on the surface of a hammerhead gravimetric chemical sensor, using 3D lithography demonstrating the ability to generate intricate 3D patterns with exceptional precision within a photoresist devoid of solvents using a UV light-sensitive polymer. The maximum sensitivity recorded was 0.8041 Hz/PPM for M-xylene utilizing Nitrogen as the carrier gas. The minimum estimated Limit of Detection (LOD) attained with the 3D-printed polymer was 64.2 ppb for M-xylene utilizing Nitrogen as the carrier gas. The 3D printed films enhance sensitivity by 2.5 times and improve the estimated LOD by 5 times compared to sensors utilizing spray-coated polymers, thereby presenting a novel opportunity for two-photon polymerization in additive manufacturing.[2025-0205]

title: Cross-Dataset Generalizability Analysis of Multimodal Self-Supervised Learning for Stress Recognition across Lab and Daily Contexts
authors: Yekta Said Can, Mohamed Benouis, Elisabeth André
abstract: Stress recognition is a key component of affect-aware systems to improve mental and physical well-being. While multimodal affect recognition systems based on physiological signals have shown promise, achieving robust generalization across different datasets remains a major challenge due to variations in stress induction protocols and labeling practices. For instance, “stress” labels can vary widely between datasets: WESAD uses the Trier Social Stress Test to induce social-evaluative stress, while SWELL-KW relies on cognitive workload tasks. Such differences in the nature and intensity of stressors, as well as inconsistencies in how labels are defined (e.g., “social stress” vs. “mental stress”), create major challenges for generalization. Prior work has explored deep transfer learning and unimodal self-supervised methods, but cross-dataset generalizability is still limited. To address this gap, we propose a multimodal self-supervised learning (SSL) framework based on contrastive objectives that learns transferable representations from unlabeled physiological signals. Unlike conventional deep transfer learning approaches, our framework does not rely on stress labels during the pretraining stage and is evaluated under a strict leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol to ensure realistic cross-subject generalization. We systematically study the impact of SSL across multiple encoder architectures, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN), ResNet34-1D, and a CNN–Transformer hybrid, enabling a systematic analysis of how different encoder architectures affect representation transferability. Experiments are conducted across three laboratory datasets (WESAD, VERBIO, AffectHRI) and two daily life datasets (SWEET, LD), covering lab-to-lab, lab-to-daily, and daily-to-lab transfer scenarios. Overall, our findings highlight multimodal self-supervised learning as an effective and label-efficient framework for improving cross-dataset generalization, particularly under realistic cross-subject and cross-context evaluation settings.

title: Privacy-Enhanced Federated Learning Algorithm Empowered by Blockchain and Game Theory
authors: Shouqiang Kang, Yulin Sun, Yuxuan Wu, Yujing Wang, Qingyan Wang, Xintao Liang
abstract: Federated learning enables participants to collaboratively train a global model through distributed training without sharing raw data. However, this distributed training is vulnerable to single-point failures, privacy leakage, and Byzantine attacks. To address these challenges simultaneously, we propose a privacy-enhanced federated learning algorithm empowered by blockchain and game theory (BGFL). First, we integrate blockchain technology into federated learning and establish a decentralized training paradigm, which effectively avoids the threat of single-point failures in centralized training. Second, we propose a privacy protection method that combines the Chinese Remainder Theorem and Shamir's Secret Sharing, providing dual privacy protection for participants in decentralized federated learning scenarios. Furthermore, leveraging the homomorphic properties of the proposed privacy-enhanced approach, a Byzantine attack defense mechanism is designed. Finally, a game-theoretic incentive mechanism is proposed to mitigate malicious behaviors during collaborative training, ensuring secure cooperation among all parties. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared to baseline methods, BGFL improves test accuracy by 0.93%–22.75%. Compared to other secret sharing-based federated learning schemes, BGFL requires only 5.91% of the computational overhead and 4.01% of the communication overhead at the same dataset scale. This enables efficient achievement of security and robustness objectives.

title: Anomaly Detection in IoT Environment Using Machine Learning: A survey
authors: Aliyu Umar, Ivan Jordanov
abstract: The explosive growth of the Internet of Things (IoT) has introduced vast amounts of data and unprecedented security challenges, making effective anomaly detection in IoT environments a critical concern. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of machine learning techniques for IoT anomaly detection, with an emphasis on their applicability, performance trade-offs, and alignment with real-world requirements. We adhere to a systematic literature review methodology (PRISMA) to identify and analyse recent advances, categorising them into supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and deep learning approaches, as well as emerging paradigms like federated learning. For each category, we discuss representative algorithms and architectures, ranging from isolation forests and one-class SVMs to deep autoencoders and graph neural networks, and highlight how they address issues such as data imbalance, concept drift, and limited labels in IoT data. We also review prominent IoT security datasets and evaluation metrics used in the field, and present illustrative results from experiments and simulations of the investigated techniques. Our comparative analysis underscores that while supervised and deep learning models achieve high accuracy under ample labelled data, unsupervised and hybrid methods offer robustness against novel attacks and evolving network behaviour. Finally, we identify open challenges (e.g., adaptive model updating, feature selection for high-dimensional IoT data, and privacy-preserving detection) and outline future research directions toward resilient, real-time anomaly detection in large-scale IoT networks.

title: TSL-ONE-S: A Real-World Thai Sign Language Dataset with Deep Learning Benchmarks
authors: Jirapong Chalotonpised, Wen-Hui Chen, Wuttichai Vijitkunsawat, Yu-Chen Lin
abstract: This paper presents TSL-ONE-S, a new word-level dataset for Thai sign language recognition, developed to address existing resource limitations and support communication for the deaf community. We present the creation, detailed annotation, and evaluation of a new dataset that aims to provide a resource for research on Thai sign language recognition. The dataset comprises 4,152 video samples covering 184 commonly used glosses, recorded from 29 signers with diverse demographics. The recordings were made in naturalistic settings with varied lighting and backgrounds, reflecting real-world conditions. Data were collected from nine schools across Thailand to ensure regional and dialectal diversity. A standardized annotation process was applied, including consistent labeling, unique signer identification, and automated video trimming using MediaPipe for pose and hand landmark detection, followed by manual validation to ensure accuracy. Signer-independent evaluations were performed using deep learning models, Inflated 3D Convolutional Neural Network (I3D), and Sign Pose-based Transformer (SPOTER), achieving top-1 classification accuracies for the TSL-ONE-S dataset of 79.69% and 80.83%, respectively. SPOTER, a pose-based transformer model, demonstrated faster training and greater robustness to environmental noise due to its skeletal-based representation.

title: Machine Learning-Assisted Synthesis of Heterogeneous Arrays via Active Geometric Design_supp1-3670284.pdf
authors: Qi Wu
abstract: Mutual coupling often leads to discrepancies between intended and synthesized array patterns due to the distortion of active element patterns (AEPs). In array synthesis, existing machine learning methods are predominantly confined to predicting AEPs for fixed structures, imposing a passive constraint that limits optimization flexibility. A holistic framework is introduced for the active geometric design of heterogeneous arrays, in which the geometric parameters of individual elements are treated as degrees of freedom alongside traditional ones. To manage the complexity associated with mutual coupling, a variational autoencoder-based surrogate model is developed to efficiently capture the mapping between diverse element structures and their radiation patterns in a latent space. Numerical comparisons indicate a 9.8× speedup in prediction over traditional methods. Furthermore, under rigorous uniform amplitude and 1-bit phase constraints, sidelobe suppression improvements of over 8 dB are achieved at scanning angles compared to homogeneous baselines, validating the efficacy of incorporating element-level geometric design into the global optimization process.

title: PES T&D announcement
authors: N/A

title: PES Transactions
authors: N/A

title: Control of Single-Phase Five-Level Current Source Grid-Connected Inverter Based on FCS-MPC
authors: Lang Li, Jianyu. Bao, Nannan Gu, Cheng Yan
abstract: For single-phase grid-connected applications, traditional Current Source Inverters (CSI) often rely on numerous switching devices and shunt branches to achieve multi-level output. This leads to increased losses, costs, and control complexity, and easily causes intermediate-level current imbalance issues. Based on the eight-switch five-level CSI, this paper proposes a six-switch five-level CSI topology characterized by four-switch high-frequency modulation in the upper arm and two-switch low-frequency commutation in the lower arm. This topology reduces one current-sharing inductor and two switching devices while maintaining five-level capability. Centering on this topology, a discrete prediction model for the inverter grid current and current-sharing inductor current is established. A multi-objective cost function for Finite Control Set Model Predictive Control (FCS-MPC) is designed to balance accurate grid current tracking with intermediatelevel current equilibrium. The control system introduces SOGI-PLL to estimate the synchronization angle, allowing the orthogonal generation of single-phase quantities and predictive calculations in the αβ\Park domain. Simulation results indicate that, under various grid current references, the inverter output current can adaptively transition between three and five levels, with the Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of the grid current meeting grid interconnection standards. The tuning of weighting coefficients allows for an adjustable compromise among current tracking, switching frequency, and intermediate current balance. This scheme is characterized by a low component count, intuitive control targets, and a high performance-to-cost ratio.

title: Optimal Control of Voltage Forming Grid Inverters by Model Predictive Control and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Daniel Weber, Dominik Schmies, Jarren Lange, Maximilian Schenke, Oliver Wallscheid
abstract: Optimal control approaches, such as model predictive control (MPC) and reinforcement learning (RL), are promising for decentralized grid applications but face challenges related to model inaccuracies and safety constraints. This work experimentally compares both methods on industrial-grade three-phase, three-level inverter hardware with an LC-filter and common neutral point, going beyond simulation-based studies. Under identical safety constraints, both controllers show similarly fast transient settling. In steady state, MPC achieves a normalized mean absolute error (with respect to the reference voltage amplitude) of 0.33%, while the RL controller reaches 0.18%. These results demonstrate that both model-based and model-free control can attain optimal performance when subjected to real-world and real-time conditions. Finally, this contribution highlights the key challenges in transferring theoretical concepts to practical power electronic inverters and offers a detailed implementation guide for both researchers and industrial practioners.

title: A Dual-Band Ridge Waveguide Bandstop Filter Using Defected Ridge Structure
authors: Ying Xue, Cheng-Yang Zhang, Shi-Chang Tang, Lei Wang, Wei Qin, Jian-Xin Chen
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, an inline dual-band ridge waveguide (RWG) bandstop filter (BSF) is proposed using a defected ridge structure. The defective ridge bandstop unit is formed by etching a stepped-width groove that transversely penetrates the middle ridge. It can be considered as a stepped-impedance slotline resonator with short-circuited ends for analysis. The dual- stopband response is generated by the fundamental mode and second harmonic of the unit, and the frequency ratio between them can be adjusted by modifying the groove dimensions. For demonstration, a prototype of a three-order dual-band BSF is fabricated and measured, showing good agreement between the simulated and measured results. The BSF centers at 9.1 and 12.78 GHz, with 3-dB bandwidths (BWs) of approximately 118 and 160 MHz, respectively. In addition, both stopbands exhibit good rejection performance, and the passbands have a low insertion loss (IL) of less than 0.5 dB.

title: Study on Insulation Degradation Characteristics and Health State Evaluation of Onboard Cables for EMUs
authors: Songru Jiang, Wanbin Zhou, Xiaojian Wang, Guozheng Zhang, Zekui Lu, Dongli Xin, Guoqiang Gao, Guangning Wu, Kui Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The inter-car jumper cable is a key component for power transmission between carriages in high-speed trains. During long-term service, it is exposed to overvoltage impulses, electromagnetic interference, and carriage displacement, forming a coupled environment of strong electric fields and repeated mechanical bending. These stresses accelerate insulation degradation and may pose significant risks to the safe operation of high-speed trains. To address this issue, this study establishes a multiphysics simulation model to investigate the distribution of the electric field and the concentration of mechanical stress under various loading conditions, identifying the regions most susceptible to insulation deterioration. Subsequently, based on degradation-influencing factors and experimental measurements, degradation-sensitive characteristic parameters are extracted, and a degradation equation is established to describe their variation with service mileage. Lastly, a multidimensional feature-based insulation evaluation model is constructed using the fuzzy analytic hierarchy process (FAHP), achieving a prediction error of only 0.861%. Combined with the microstructural evolution of the main insulation layer, the study further clarifies the coupled electrical-mechanical degradation mechanism. This work reveals the key factors governing insulation degradation, proposes a quantitative evaluation method, and offers practical guidance for the operation and maintenance of inter-car jumper cables.

title: Estimation of Pinus taeda L. volume using the Random Forest algorithm and hyperspectral image in southern Brazil
authors: Fábio Rodrigues Spiazzi, Veraldo Liesenberg, Leonardo Josoé Biffi, Marcos Benedito Schimalski
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: Knowledge about the production of a forest area is essential for planning management activities, and machine learning techniques applied to remote sensing data have contributed to obtaining indirect production estimates at a reduced cost. The objective of this work was to evaluate the use of the Random Forest Regressor (RFR) algorithm in estimating the volume of Pinus taeda L. by analyzing hyperspectral data from the EnMAP orbital sensor. The model adjustment results were considered satisfactory on the test data, with a coefficient of determination ($\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$) of $\mathbf{0. 5 8 6}$, a root mean squared error (RMSE) of $69.77 \mathrm{~m}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{ha}^{-1}$, and a mean absolute error (MAE) of $53.75 \mathrm{~m}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{ha}^{-1}$. The spectral band groups that best explain the model are located within the near-infrared (NIR: $\sim 734.65 \mathrm{~nm}$ to $\sim 778.56 \mathrm{~nm}$) and shortwave-infrared (SWIR: $\sim 1780.22 \mathrm{~nm}$ to $1986.45 \mathrm{~nm})$ regions. Finally, a production volume prediction map ($\mathrm{m}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{ha}^{-1}$) was generated and compared with the average volume ($\mathrm{m}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{ha}^{-1}$) of the inventory. There was no statistically significant difference between the inventory data and the volume data predicted by the map, confirming that the RFR has potential application in estimating the volume of Pinus taeda L. in southern Brazil.

title: From Spectra to Semantics: An ontology-based Model of Spectral Observations Results
authors: Fernando Roda, Francisco Nemiña
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: It is evident that there is an absence of a unified and meaningful scheme for the storage and annotation of spectral data. Consequently, a significant amount of spectral observation data remains isolated in data silos, with few opportunities for reuse or integration by third parties. The present paper introduces an ontology-driven conceptual model aligned with the Semantic Sensor Network Ontology (SSN) to formally describe spectral observations from remote sensing instruments. The model addresses key limitations in existing frameworks by explicitly representing the internal structure of spectral data, such as the relationship between wavelength intervals and measured intensities, using description logic-based constructs. The proposed ontology integrates design principles from the Quantities, Units, Dimensions and Data Types (QUDT) ontology, and introduces new classes to describe the basis of spectral measurements, thereby supporting multispectral and hyperspectral data. By enabling machinereadable representations of spectra, this framework enhances semantic interoperability, facilitates advanced queries, and supports use cases such as spectral data fusion, validation, and measurements transformation across sensors. Implementation examples include realworld instruments such as the ASD FieldSpec 4 HiRes spectroradiometer and Landsat 8 OLI sensor, which demonstrate the utility of the model. The framework contributes to the development of FAIR-compliant, semantically rich infrastructures for Earth observation data.

title: The Key Technology of Dynamic Analysis and Intelligent Interaction Module of Power Safety
authors: Jing Liu, Jianxin Liu, Weihang Wang, Qingchao Xu, Pu Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: In order to meet the real-time dynamic monitoring requirements of the power operation environment, a Fast Region based Convolutional Neural Network (Faster R-CNN) network is studied, and an online learning mechanism, adaptive feature extraction module, and dynamic anchor box generation strategy are introduced to design a power safety dynamic analysis algorithm. Simultaneously building an intelligent interaction module to achieve risk warning, information push, and user feedback collection. The results showed that the proposed algorithm achieved accuracy rates of 96.8 %, 95.2 %, and 94.5 % in personnel, equipment, and hazard detection, respectively, with a comprehensive detection accuracy of 95.5 %, which is higher than the comparative algorithms; The average false alarm rate in complex scenarios is 4.0 %, which is much lower than other algorithms. In the intelligent interaction module, the success rate of multi-channel push is high, with an App push success rate of 99.0 %. This research method is helpful in promoting the intelligent process of safety management in the power industry, reducing operational risks, and improving the overall level of operational safety.

title: Research and Implementation of Distributed Interference Coordination Algorithms for Heterogeneous Networks Targeting Edge Intelligence
authors: Xu Li
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: A distributed interference coordination framework integrating graph-theoretic modeling and reinforcement learningbased resource partitioning is developed for heterogeneous wireless networks enabling edge intelligence. The proposed approach abstracts a multi-tier network as a weighted interference graph, enabling scalable representation of spatial and spectral conflicts among base stations and edge servers. Based on Markov decision process, adaptive power and bandwidth allocation is based on hardware constraints and quality of service. The system's optimization objective jointly maximizes network throughput and AI task completion rates while maintaining fairness and reliability. Simulation results in dense network scenarios demonstrate that this method significantly enhances aggregate system throughput, AI task completion rate, and resource utilization efficiency compared to conventional static and uncoordinated benchmarks. The framework demonstrates strong operational stability and convergence in dynamic traffic patterns and high user mobility patterns. The results show the effectiveness and feasibility of distributed, learning-driven interference coordination in the deployment of advanced edge intelligence in heterogeneous wireless environments.

title: Genetic Algorithm Based Radial Basis Function Neural Network PID Control System for a Quadrotor UAV
authors: Kailun Liu
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: The quadrotor UAV represents an underactuated, multi-input, and strongly coupled system, making manual PID parameter tuning inefficient and suboptimal. To address this, this paper designs an adaptive PID control system utilizing a Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural network for online parameter adjustment. A hybrid optimization strategy integrating Genetic Algorithm (GA) and Back Propagation (BP) algorithm is employed to pre-optimize the initial weights, centers, and widths of the RBF network, effectively combining global search with local fine-tuning. Simulation results in MATLAB demonstrate that the proposed GA-BP optimized RBF-PID controller achieves convergence within 8 iterations and reduces overshoot by over 60 % compared to conventional PID control. The system exhibits rapid dynamic response, minimal steady-state error, and enhanced robustness, confirming its superior control accuracy and adaptability for complex quadrotor systems.

title: 2SRL: A Sequential Deep Reinforcement Learning Framework for Two-Stage Stochastic Programming with Recourse
authors: Bangde Cao, Shuyu Zhang, Hang Liu, Zhixuan Chen, Yilin Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Two-stage stochastic programming can characterize resource allocation and risk trade-offs under uncertainty via a “here-and-now decision + recourse decision” structure. How-ever, when the scenario set is massive and the recourse stage is complex (e.g., involving hard constraints or combinatorial structures), traditional exact or decomposition-based algorithms often incur high computational overhead and fail to meet the latency requirements of online decision-making. To address this issue, we propose a deep reinforcement learning based two-stage reinforcement learning framework (Two-Stage Reinforcement Learning, 2SRL) built upon the classical two-stage stochastic optimization modeling paradigm. Specifically, we train a first-stage agent and a second-stage agent separately, and adopt a sequential training mechanism to alleviate the non-stationarity in multi-agent training, enabling the first-stage policy to leverage second-stage feedback for end-to-end coupled learning between “decision” and “recourse”. Numerical experiments, conducted under a unified software-hardware environment, compare 2SRL with Gurobi, SDDiP, the PECH heuristic, and a random policy. The results show that 2SRL can return high-quality solutions in sub-second time: under the training setting of #items= 10, on test sets with #sc= 50 and #sc= 500, 2SRL achieves speedups of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.7\times 10^{5}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.6\times 10^{4}$</tex> over Gurobi, respectively, while maintaining an optimality gap of about 6.6%-10.2% (mean ≈ 7.53%); under the training setting of #items= 20, when #sc= 1000, the solving time can still be reduced to the 0.2 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$s$</tex> level (speedup over Gurobi <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 3.2\times 10^{4}$</tex>), with an average optimality gap of about 10.25%. Overall, 2SRL exhibits a pronounced preference for low latency in the “solution quality-time cost” trade-off, making it suitable for online stochastic decision-making scenarios with stringent real-time response requirements.

title: Mapping burned areas in Cerrado protected areas (2000–2020): a comparative analysis of Landsat classification, Mapbiomas fire, and MCD64A1
authors: Tânia Beatriz Hoffmann, Egidio Arai, Yosio Edemir Shimabukuro, Marcos Adami
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: This study maps burned areas in Brasília National Park (BNP) and Chapada dos Veadeiros National Park (CVNP), including their 10 km buffer zones, from 2000 to 2020. A supervised classification was applied using the Random Forest algorithm in Google Earth Engine (GEE) with Landsat imagery and spectral indices. The combination of NBR2 and NDVI achieved the highest classification accuracy in 65% of the evaluated years compared to NBR2 and NDWI. NDVI proved effective for detecting burned areas, especially for capturing structural changes in vegetation. Annual mosaics generated from the standard deviation of minimum and maximum pixel values improved the detection of spectral variability related to burned area dynamics. The largest burned areas were observed in 2007 and 2010, considering park and buffer zones, with $310.37 \mathrm{~km}^{2}$ and $433.45 \mathrm{~km}^{2}$ in BNP, and $1,566.59 \mathrm{~km}^{2}$ and $1,457.42 \mathrm{~km}^{2}$ in CVNP. Spatial patterns differed between parks: fires were more frequent in the buffer zone of BNP, while in CVNP most fires occurred within park boundaries. Field validation in 2019 confirmed the accuracy of the mapped areas but also revealed limitations in detecting small burned scars. The historical data highlights burned area variation over time, supporting discussions on integrated fire management and strategies to reduce large wildfires.

title: University Students' Voices on Large Language Models: Perceptions, Benefits, and Challenges in Academic Writing
authors: Hui Ma, Xueshan Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: This qualitative study examines the impact of Large Language Models (LLMs) on academic writing among English as a Second Language (ESL) students at a top-tier Chinese university. Through longitudinal surveys and thematic analysis of participants' responses collected at four stages of an LLMs-integrated writing course, the research explores students' evolving perceptions. The findings indicate that students perceive LLMs as multifunctional collaborators, fulfilling three key roles: writing assistants that streamline technical aspects of composition, virtual tutors that provide iterative scaffolding, and brainstorming partners that facilitate ideation. These roles collectively enhance writing efficiency, improve output quality, and offer affective support. However., challenges include technical limitations (e.g., generic outputs, factual inaccuracies), over-reliance risks (e.g., diminished critical thinking), and disciplinary mismatches. To effectively incorporate LLMs into writing pedagogy, the study recommends systematically embedding LLMs literacy instruction that encompasses prompt formulation techniques, identification of potential biases, and ethical usage principles. Educators play a crucial mediating role by critically evaluating and adapting LLMs -generated content to align with disciplinary writing standards., while reserving higher-order cognitive tasks for student development. These findings suggest a balanced approach: using LLMs' technical strengths while preserving the core teaching focus that nurtures students' analytical thinking and original expression through consistent human guidance.

title: A Proactive and Hierarchical Workflow Scheduling Scheme for Cloud-Edge-End Collaboration
authors: Chenlu Wang, Yuhuai Peng, Yi Gong
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on High Performance Computing, Big Data and Communication Engineering (ICHBC)
abstract: Cloud-edge-end collaborative computing is emerging as a foundational paradigm for large-scale Artificial Intelligence (AI) applications. However, the interplay of complex Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) dependencies, fierce resource competition, and dynamic system states leads to myopic scheduling decisions, resulting in long-tailed makespans, low task completion rates, and load imbalance. To address these challenges, we propose the Hierarchical, Ripple-aware Proactive Scheduling (HARPS) scheme. HARPS employs a hierarchical strategy that distinguishes tasks by their importance. For critical tasks pivotal to global performance, its Foresight-driven Path Allocation (FPA) algorithm uses limited-depth path planning and soft resource reservation to preemptively mitigate bottlenecks. For the majority of non-critical tasks, the Ripple-aware Trade-off Placement (RTP) algorithm leverages a multi-dimensional cost function to balance immediate efficiency, ripple effects, and system load. Extensive simulations show that under high-load saturation, when the completion rates of baseline algorithms fall below 40%, HARPS sustains a rate above 83%. It achieves a relative performance gain of up to $5 \times$ compared to baselines, significantly enhancing the reliability and efficiency of the system.

title: RAG Technology for Reliable Medical Retrieval and Hallucination Mitigation
authors: Mengze Wu
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: The application of large language models (LLMs) in the medical field is prone to hallucinations because large models predict the next word in the form of probabilities, making hallucinations inevitable. Furthermore, both fine-tuning and LoRA fine-tuning are extremely costly. This study uses retrievalaugmented generation (RAG) technology to provide local models with professional context through an external knowledge base, thereby reducing hallucination output. This experiment constructs a medical Q&A system comprising a retrieval module and a generation module. The retrieval module uses Chromedb to retrieve and select the top-k relevant fragments, while the generation module concatenates the retrieval results with the query and inputs them into a large model to generate answers. The results of this experiment show that, compared with the baseline model of local deepseek:1-7b, the median cosine similarity between the relevant paragraphs and the query increased by 17%, and the Euclidean distance decreased by approximately 18%. After introducing the confidence threshold, the number of illusory answers to 30 test questions decreased by approximately 30%. It can be seen that RAG significantly improves the accuracy and reliability of Q&A, greatly reducing the probability of hallucinations and expanding the scope of LLM applications in professional fields. However, since the model inference logic has not been changed, so hallucination issues may still occur. At the same time, the system lacks a multi-round dialogue mechanism, and the recall strategy needs to be optimized.

title: Adapting Active Learning to Improve Hyperspectral Image Classification within Supervised Learning
authors: Victor Hugo Ayma Quirita, Victor Andres Ayma Quirita, A. O. P. Gilson Costa, Antonio Plaza Miguel
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: The performance of hyperspectral image classification (HIC) models strongly depends on the informativeness and representativeness of the training data, which directly impacts classification accuracy. Active learning (AL) has been introduced as a strategy to enhance classification performance by selecting informative and representative samples from unlabeled data and incorporating them into the training process. Although AL has shown promising results in various applications, it requires an oracle to label new data. In this work, we eliminate the need for an oracle and adapt the principles of AL to the supervised learning paradigm. We integrate key concepts from AL into supervised learning by iteratively updating a supervised classifier with subsets of labeled and (potentially) informative data extracted from a fully labeled dataset. Experiments conducted on real hyperspectral data demonstrate that our method outperforms conventional supervised learning when implemented with a standard neural network architecture.

title: TCN-GCN: A Hybrid Temporal-Graph Network with Multi-Feature Fusion for Radar Signal Classification
authors: Jiyang Li, Baozhong Zhang, Ziwei Song, Haodan Lei, Haimei Du, Yuan Yao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Traditional radar signal classification struggles in complex electromagnetic environments due to reliance on single features and insufficient temporal modeling. This paper introduces a robust classification framework combining intra-pulse multi-feature fusion with a TCN-GCN hybrid architecture to address these issues. Specifically, we employ a gated attention mechanism to integrate time-frequency, phase, and bispectrum features, while the TCN-GCN model captures complex temporal characteristics and inter-signal dependencies. Empirical results show the proposed method outperforms existing techniques, delivering high classification accuracy and strong resilience against low SNR, pulse overlap, and signal loss.

title: End-to-End Shutter Design and Reconstruction for Dynamic Scene Imaging
authors: Hans Garcia, Sebastian Ardila, Henry Arguello
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: High-quality video acquisition is essential for emerging applications such as remote sensing, autonomous driving, and augmented reality. However, the sequential readout inherent to CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) sensors employing rolling shutters introduces severe geometric distortions, particularly during rapid scene motion. Traditional distortion-correction methods either rely on computationally intensive post-processing or necessitate costly global shutter hardware, resulting in significant trade-offs between accuracy, complexity, and cost. To address these limitations, we propose an end-to-end optimization framework integrating a custom optical layer represented by a compact, learnable $M \times N$ mask and a neural reconstruction network. By jointly optimizing optical acquisition and digital correction, our method significantly reduces distortion artifacts, achieving up to 4.3 dB PSNR improvement over a conventional rolling shutter fixed acquisition sequence in the test set. Our approach provides a practical and effective solution for high-speed, distortion-free scene capture.

title: Large-Scale Network Traffic Classification Using Graph Clustering-Enhanced E-GraphSAGE
authors: Yuxin Ma, Jun Ma, Yan Fan
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Network traffic classification is a key technology for intrusion detection systems, but traditional methods face bottlenecks such as high computational complexity and large memory consumption when handling large-scale graph-structured data. To address this challenge, this paper proposes the Cluster-E-GraphSAGE framework, which innovatively combines edge-aware graph neural networks with the METIS graph partitioning algorithm to achieve efficient processing of large-scale network traffic graphs through an efficient subgraph partitioning strategy and edge-aware message passing mechanism. The framework supports multi-directed graph structures, completely preserving edge multiplicity and feature attributes, and significantly reduces computational complexity and memory overhead through edge-based subgraph partitioning. Experiments on the standard NF-BoT-IoT and NF-ToN-IoT datasets show that Cluster-E-GraphSAGE performs particularly well in multi-class classification tasks, achieving F1 scores of 92.82% and 81.32% respectively, which are 11.23% and 23.6% improvements over baseline methods. Meanwhile, it reduces memory usage by 4-7 times and GPU memory by 8-11 times. The research results verify that the method maintains high classification accuracy while significantly reducing memory overhead in resource-constrained environments, demonstrating its application potential in real-time intrusion detection scenarios.

title: A Lightweight Sequence-Based Model for Folding Motif Classification
authors: Zhuoyan Li
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: When experimental structures are unavailable or large deep models are impractical, sequence-only fold recognition remains a viable alternative. We study a lightweight, alignment-free classifier for five SCOPe 2.08 β-fold classes (ASTRAL-95: b.40, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{b}. 42, ~\mathrm{b}. 43, ~\mathrm{b}. 47, ~\mathrm{b}. 50; \mathrm{N}=917$</tex>). We evaluate interpretable sequence descriptors-amino acid composition (AAC, 20D), grouped AAC, class-based bigrams, and hydrophobic-polar (HP) features (4D)—with a Random Forest classifier. Among single-feature sets, AAC performed best; adding HP features yielded consistent gains, whereas bigrams did not help on this dataset. To mitigate class imbalance, SMOTE was applied within training folds. The AAC+HP (24D) model reached <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.868 \pm 0.018$</tex> accuracy and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.849 \pm$</tex> 0.021 macro-F1 under stratified 5-fold cross-validation; on an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$80 / 20$</tex> hold-out split it achieved 0.864 accuracy, and the recall of the minority class b. 43 rose from 0.27 to 0.59 with SMOTE. Feature-importance analyses (Gini/permutation) highlighted HP segment statistics and several AAC dimensions (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$G, C$</tex>, aromatic residues), supporting biological interpretability. Finally, adding a compact set of sequence-derived structural proxies (STR, 14D) to AAC + HP left macro-F1 unchanged (paired t-test <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p} \approx 0.97$</tex>), indicating that global composition plus HP statistics capture most separability while keeping computation low.

title: Virtues in Action: Exploring Virtue Disclosure in Children’s Expressive Coding Narratives
authors: Avia Ben-Ari, Rinat B. Rosenberg-Kima
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: This study examines how novice learners express virtue-based dispositions through their Expressive Coding (EC) narratives, exploring how coding engagement may reveal moral dimensions in learning processes. Building on Bers’ Coding as Another Language (CAL) curriculum, EC integrates constructionist coding instruction with Expressive Arts Therapy (EAT)-informed principles, wherein projective storytelling allows the expression of children’s inner experiences, while learning basic coding skills on the programming platform ScratchJr. Twenty third-grade children with no prior programming experience participated in a five-session EC intervention, wherein they created personal stories following predefined prompts. A mixed-methods analysis combining the Creative Coding Rubric (CCR) with a directed content analysis informed by Bers’ Palette of Virtues and an inductive thematic analysis were conducted. Quantitative CCR results showed most children reached the Advanced proficiency level, demonstrating meaningful creative-coding knowledge. Qualitative analyses revealed recurrent expression of perseverance (70%), generosity (40%), and optimism (35%) across children’s artifacts, along with courage, loyalty, respect, and faith as emergent virtues additional to those included in CAL. Findings indicate that EC supports both computational and moral growth, positioning coding as a projective space for moral exploration. Linking CCR dimensions with virtue-related manifestations, the study proposes to extend creative coding assessment as a potential proxy for virtue enactment. Overall, EC further demonstrates how coding-based storytelling may nurture children’s developing moral selves alongside foundational coding knowledge acquisition.

title: Revocable and Flexible Privacy-Preserving Data Computing with Bilateral Access Control for Cloud-Fog-Assisted EHR Systems
authors: Mengting Yao, Jian Weng, Junjie Huang, Hongkai Liu, Jia-Nan Liu, Zhiquan Liu, Jiasi Weng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Cloud-fog-assisted electronic health record (EHR) systems offer promising solutions for large-scale medical data storage and processing. However, they also raise critical privacy concerns, particularly regarding secure computation over sensitive data, fine-grained bilateral access control, dynamic revocation, and decryption key exposure. Existing cryptographic primitives, such as functional encryption and matchmaking encryption, address some of these challenges individually but fail to offer a unified solution. In this work, we design a revocable and privacy-preserving data computing system with bilateral access control (RPDC-BAC) for cloud-fog-assisted EHR sharing by proposing a novel cryptographic primitive, called server-aided revocable attribute-based matchmaking functional encryption (SR-AB-MFE). Specifically, the proposed scheme supports expressive bilateral access control and computation over encrypted data. In addition, it incorporates time-evolving decryption keys and a server-aided revocation mechanism to mitigate key exposure and efficiently revoke users. To further reduce receiver-side overhead, fog nodes assist in ciphertext authentication and partial decryption. We formally define the proposed primitive and prove its security under static assumptions. Finally, extensive experimental results demonstrate the efficiency and practicality of our design.
