title: Single- and Dual-Band Frequency-Agile Dielectric Resonator Filters
authors: Kot-Lam Chu, Yuan Chun Li, Di-Si Wu, Quan Xue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Single- and dual-band frequency-agile dielectric resonator (DR) filters are proposed in this article. A printed circuit board (PCB) with varactors is attached to the bottom of the DR. It has two functions: first, the boundary condition of the bottom side of DR is reconstructed as the electric wall and two resonant half-mode are obtained. Second, the tuning elements and bias circuit are easily embedded with the 3-D structure. After that, the two resonant modes can be controlled independently. Moreover, a cross feeding line is utilized to excite the two modes simultaneously. Based on the proposed tunable dual-half-mode DR, a single- and dual-band reconfigurable filters are designed. The measured results demonstrate that, only using two resonators, the two passbands of the DR filter can be tuned independently and the roll-off rate of the passband can be maintained sharp in the tuning range. Benefiting from the electrical tuning scheme, the tuning speed of the filter is high compared with the mechanical tuning.

title: Conference Calendar
authors: N/A

title: A Machine Learning-Based Personalized Recommendation Method for Online Educational Resources in Sports
authors: Jianhua Chen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Traditional online education resource recommendations rely on manual rules or simple user behavior statistics, which have problems such as low accuracy, difficulty in handling sparse data, and insufficient personalization. To this end, a machine learning based personalized recommendation method for sports online education resources is proposed: first, users' multidimensional preference information (browsing, learning, interaction, etc.) is captured, and interest features are extracted using clustering and classification algorithms; Construct an interest matching matrix again, input resource keywords and user behavior feature vectors into the matrix, and establish a recommendation model; Finally, generate a personalized list based on the level of interest. Experiments have shown that this method has higher F-Measure values than traditional methods, lower MAE values, and recommendations that better fit user preferences. It has significant advantages in handling sparse data and improving accuracy.

title: Cliva-Med: A Lightweight Vision-Language Model for Medical Visual Question Answering
authors: Juni Stefanus Santoso, Xiaohan Ma, Kehui Song, Rize Jin, Xuguang Qiu, Hongbo Zhao
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: While vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promise in healthcare and biomedical fields, challenges persist concerning their computational requirements and accessibility. This paper introduces Cliva-Med, a lightweight medical VLM. It consists of a vision encoder, a lightweight large language model backbone, and a cross-modality projector. It is trained through a process involving the reformulation of a large multimodal biomedical dataset for visual-language alignment and instruction fine-tuning with a smaller dataset to enhance understanding of medical concepts and scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that Cliva-Med achieves performance comparable to or exceeding current state-of-the-art models in closed-ended medical question answering and image classification tasks, while requiring only 30–50% of their parameters. Qualitative analyses further demonstrate Cliva-Med's strong logical reasoning and enhanced multimodal capabilities. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/jun1299/Cliva-Med.

title: Supporting Argentina’s forest production through satellite remote sensing
authors: Matías Ernesto Barber, Daniel Castellano Francucci, Axel von Müller, Inés Del Valle Asís
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: This paper aims to describe the rationale of forest Above Ground Biomass (AGB) and related Aboveground Carbon Stock (ACS) products developed with satellite remote sensing. Optical and radar imagery were synergistically combined to (1) build a classification map of vegetation species; (2) build a regression of cross-polarized, angle-normalized radar backscattering coefficient ($\gamma_{H V}^{0}$) with ground surveys for each class; (3) apply the corresponding regressions to the entire vegetation class map to retrieve AGB and ACS. The scope of these products is focused on two of Argentina’s most densely forested provinces: Misiones in the northwestermost part and Chubut in the south. The classes involve native (tropical and temperate) and implanted forests and the ground survey sampled homogeneous stands of each, totalizing 193 data measurements of AGB. ACS was derived from AGB using published dasometric relations on a species-basis. The output of each product consists of five layers, three of which define the lower and upper limits of an 80% confidence level interval and its median value. The other two are ancillary layers. The products are freely available online at the National Commission on Space Activities (CONAE) website [1].

title: Study of Imperfect Key to Characterise the Security of Optical Encryption
authors: Lingfei Zhang, Haiqing Zhang, Wenxuan Hou
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: In conventional symmetric encryption, it is common for the encryption/decryption key to be reused for multiple plaintexts. This gives rise to the concept of a known-plaintext attack. In optical image encryption systems, such as double random phase encoding (DRPE), this is also the case. With optical encryption, if one knows a plaintext-ciphertext pair, one can carry out a known-plaintext attack more efficiently than a bruteforce attack, using heuristics based on phase retrieval or simulated annealing. However, it is more likely that an attacker will find an imperfect encryption/decryption key using such heuristics. Such an imperfect key will work for the known plaintext-ciphertext pair, but not an arbitrary unseen plaintext-ciphertext pair using the original key. In this paper, we illustrate the problem and attempt to characterise the increase in security it affords optical encryption.

title: Multistage Algorithm for Automatic Mining Dredges Detection on Amazonian Rivers using SAR
authors: Caio Henrique De Felice, Elcio Hideiti Shiguemori, Rafael Lemos Paes, Daniel Ferreira Martins, Milton Dos Santos
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: Illegal gold mining in remote regions of the Brazilian Amazon poses great environmental and security threats. Developing detection systems for mining dredges is hampered by limited labeled data, often due to adverse weather. Despite the urgency, few studies have explored automated approaches using SAR imagery under low-data regimes. This work presents an automated pipeline for detecting mining dredges in ICEYE SAR images. The method begins by splitting full-scene GRD images into chips, which were classified using a Prototypical Network trained via Few-Shot Learning. A local range filter is then applied to enhance contrast, followed by threshold-based blob detection to localize potential dredges. The approach achieved recall rates between 79.7% and 93.2%, demonstrating competitive performance. Although it cannot yet distinguish dredges from other small vessels, its full automation and scalability make it suitable for large-scale monitoring of regions at risk of illegal mining.

title: Research Topics and Future Trends on Creative Thinking Skill Teaching and Learning in AIGC Age Based on Bibliometric Analysis
authors: Phetsavanh Sengmeuang, Chenyu Xu, Lan Wu, Thattavanh Sengmeuang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: The advent of generative artificial intelligence (AIGC) technologies such as ChatGPT has sparked renewed interest in fostering creative thinking skills in primary and secondary education. This study conducted a bibliometric analysis of 74 articles published between 2020 and 2025 in the Web of Science, using Cite Space for data visualization and network mapping. The analysis explored co-citation networks, institutional collaborations, keyword co-occurrence, and keyword clustering to uncover key research themes and emerging trends in AIGC-supported creative learning. Findings revealed eight major research clusters, including ethical concerns in AI-assisted learning, AI-driven pedagogical innovation, and human-AI cocreation, which positions AIGC as a creative collaborator rather than just a tool. Other prominent clusters addressed prompt engineering, cognitive offloading, and mental effort in learning processes, and domain-specific applications in medical and dental education, which require adaptive, real-world creativity. The study concludes that AIGC, if designed and implemented ethically and with a clear educational purpose, can be an effective mechanism for promoting creativity skills in the future.

title: An Efficient Swin Transformer Architecture Based on Grouped Query Attention
authors: Li Wang, Zhong Ma
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: This paper presents SwinGQA, a novel visual architecture that integrates Grouped Query Attention (GQA) with the Swin Transformer. To address the issue of high GPU memory consumption in traditional Swin Transformers during dense prediction tasks, the proposed method effectively reduces memory usage by grouping query heads and sharing key-value heads within each group, all while preserving linear computational complexity. This advancement enables the deployment of computation-intensive visual image processing algorithms on resource-constrained embedded platforms. Experimental results demonstrate that the key-value heads sharing mechanism can effectively improve model inference speed. Compared with the Multi-Query Attention (MQA) approach where all query heads share a single set of key-value heads, SwinGQA achieves optimized speed performance that strikes a balance between Multi-Head Attention (MHA) and MQA, providing users with more flexible architectural choices.

title: Access Control Policies Specification and Analysis for Multi-Institutional Collaborative Projects
authors: Abhimanyu Chawla, Mahmoud Abdelgawad, Indrakshi Ray
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Collaboration and Internet Computing (CIC)
abstract: Most of the scientific research is collaborative in nature, often involving multiple institutions, possibly spanning multiple countries, with a requirement to follow various institutional and legal regulations. Such a collaboration is often dynamic in nature, with users and organizations joining or leaving, data and software resources created or destroyed at the various stages in the scientific workflow. Access policies are set-up manually, often in an ad-hoc manner. This creates two potential problems. First, there is often times over-provisioning of resources. Second, an entity belonging to an external organization often has access to sensitive system resources of an institution. Over-provisioning of resources can lead to security breaches. Moreover, privileges assigned may continue to exist even after the user has left the collaboration. In addition, access to a specific task in a workflow may necessitate root access to a server. Thus, there is a potential for compromising the confidentiality and integrity of the data, the program, and the system hosting them. A dynamic access control model is needed, whose policies must be correctly specified and automatically enforced to protect the research security of this scientific collaboration. We propose the use of NIST Next Generation Access Control (NGAC) model for protecting the confidentiality and integrity of the research and the associated infrastructure for individual stakeholders in a collaborative project. We describe how to map security requirements into NGAC policies that govern the workflow and the system operations. We define a set of desirable properties for the NGAC policies that protect the workflow and describe consistency checkers for verifying them. Workflows are defined as a set of high-level coordinated tasks; these tasks are executed by low-level system operations. We propose a refinement checker that verifies that a user executing an abstract task does indeed have the permissions to access the low-level system operations that are implementing the task. We demonstrate our approach on various usecases from scientific research workflows and show its effectiveness. Our approach ensures that access control requirements are correctly and consistently specified and enforced at the various levels of abstraction in a dynamic and collaborative environment.

title: Construction and Application Path of Core Competence Framework for Vocational Undergraduate Tourism Management Majors Driven by Artificial Intelligence: A Qualitative Study Based on Grounded Theory
authors: Shihao Ye
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Against the backdrop of the digital transformation of vocational education and the upgrading of tourism industry, vocational undergraduate tourism management majors face the urgent need to cultivate teachers' composite competence adapted to artificial intelligence (AI) application. Taking tourism management majors in higher vocational colleges in Yichang as the research object, this study adopts grounded theory to conduct in-depth interviews with 50 teachers, 8 enterprise experts, and 50 students, and collects secondary data such as teaching files and enterprise project documents. Through three-level coding (open coding, axial coding, and selective coding), a three-dimensional competence framework of “basic competence-core competencedevelopmental competence” is constructed, including AI tool operation, AI integration into characteristic courses, and industry-education collaborative application. Furthermore, the study designs supporting application paths such as AI teaching case libraries, competence evaluation scales, and industryeducation collaboration mechanisms. The research enriches the practical cases of teacher competence research in vocational undergraduate tourism management majors, and provides operable solutions for the cultivation of AI-adapted teachers in similar majors, which is of great significance for promoting the integration of AI and tourism education and supporting the highquality development of vocational undergraduate education.

title: Optimization of Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Cluster Communication Nodes Based on Deep Q-Learning Networks
authors: Liyuan Huang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of communication-oriented UAV deployment in typical linear UAV ad-hoc networks. The UAV network consists of a small number of high-performance computing UAV nodes at the upper layer and a high-density swarm of normal UAVs at the lower layer. The aircraft network remains relatively stable, while these nodes can form various types of cluster network topologies. Some common UAVs are selected as normal nodes, while others are chosen as leader nodes. Leader nodes receive data from normal nodes and transmit it to gateway nodes to relay cluster information to the upper layer. It is assumed that UAVs that carry the various information need to send data to gateway nodes via routes that may experience link failures. Our objective is to guide one or more UAVs to dynamically adjust their positions to compensate for link failures caused by the dynamic changes of cluster. We design a novel queuing model called “multi-hop queuing network” to model and analyze the achievable QoS performance through multi-hop queues. For dynamic cluster topologies and time-varying link conditions, we propose a Deep Q-learning (DQN) model to determine the optimal links between UAV nodes and employ optimization algorithms to locally fine-tune the positions of cluster. The network status of the ad-hoc network is determined by calculating the instantaneous network throughput. The UAV link selection based on DQN is computed by a computing UAV equipped with the capability to maintain the cluster topology graph, while the optimization process is executed at the UAV side. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed localization model in improving network throughput efficiency.

title: Adaptive Anomaly Detection for Non-Stationary Time-Series: A Continual Learning Framework with Dynamic Distribution Monitoring
authors: Yingxin Ou, Sumeng Huang, Feiyang Wang, Kan Zhou, Yingyi Shu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Non-stationary time-series data poses significant challenges for anomaly detection systems due to evolving patterns and distribution shifts that render traditional static models ineffective. This paper presents a novel continual learning framework that integrates dynamic distribution monitoring mechanisms to enable adaptive anomaly detection in nonstationary environments. The proposed framework employs a dual-module architecture consisting of a distribution drift detector and an adaptive learning component. The distribution drift detector utilizes statistical hypothesis testing to identify temporal shifts in data distributions, while the adaptive learning module employs rehearsal-based continual learning strategies with dynamic memory management to maintain model performance across evolving patterns. We introduce a hybrid loss function that balances stability and plasticity, preventing catastrophic forgetting while enabling rapid adaptation to new distributions. Experimental results demonstrate an average F1score improvement of 11.3% over the best-performing baseline, highlighting the robustness and adaptability of the proposed framework under non-stationary conditions while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for real-time applications.

title: Wildfire-Aware Protection: Redefining Grid Engineering for a New Risk Landscape
authors: Farrokh Aminifar, Scott Hayes, Jonathan Sykes
abstract: Wildfires have grown in frequency, scale, and impact, driven by climate change, expanding vegetation, and the spread of development into wildland–urban interface (WUI) areas. The WUI, where human development meets wildland vegetation, is especially vulnerable as it combines flammable fuels with dense infrastructure and population. Protecting these zones is critical because fires in WUI regions pose heightened risks to lives, property, and grid assets. Some of the most destructive wildfires in California have been attributed to distribution and transmission systems, underscoring the need for a system wide approach to mitigation. While electric infrastructure accounts for only a fraction of ignitions, these events often occur during extreme weather and can produce disproportionately large and destructive fires. This makes it imperative for utilities to revisit grid design, engineering practices, and protection strategies to reduce ignition risk under evolving environmental conditions. Addressing this challenge requires a coordinated effort that extends beyond utility boundaries, recognizing wildfire mitigation as a public priority supported by agencies, regulators, and communities. A robust, multi-stakeholder ecosystem, spanning research, manufacturing, utilities, and policy bodies, must work together to accelerate the development and deployment of effective solutions. Hierarchical policies should guide utilities in redefining operational processes, adopting advanced technologies, strengthening standards, and integrating wildfire risk reduction into every aspect of system planning and operation. This paper reviews wildfire incidents from multiple authoritative sources, examines the physical mechanisms by which electrical faults can ignite fires, and considers engineering, protection, and monitoring measures to reduce this risk. The discussion emphasizes proactive design and physical system strategies to move beyond incremental improvements, aiming for a fire-aware and resilient grid. Achieving this vision requires coordinated policy, targeted funding, and active stakeholder collaboration to address the challenge holistically.

title: PhonePredictor: Bridging the Semantic Gap Between Neural Audio Codecs and Phonetic Sequences in Carefully Crafted Scenarios
authors: Xinning Song, Rui Wang, Zhihua Wei
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Discrete representations in neural audio codecs are prone to non-linear distortions under acoustic degradation, leading to severe phoneme confusion in Voice Conversion tasks. This paper introduces a standardized framework to diagnose these linguistic deviations. We construct a robust Phoneme Confusion Benchmark containing 1,700 carefully crafted diagnostic samples targeting stress shift, numerical confusion, and abnormal pauses. Furthermore, we propose PhonePredictor, a Conformer-CTC based mapping model designed to decode phonemes directly from latent codes. Experiments demonstrate that our model achieves a Phoneme Error Rate (PER) of 8.39 % on the VCTK dataset, significantly outperforming MLP, RNN and Transformer baselines. The proposed framework provides a transparent and quantifiable tool for evaluating the content consistency of codecbased voice conversion systems.

title: MTL-YOLO: Multi-Task Learning-Based YOLO for Detecting Bone Irregular and Discontinuity
authors: Shifeng Shang, Mingwei Cao, Haifeng Zhao
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: With the rapid advancement of medical imaging technology, deep learning-based object detection methods have demonstrated significant potential in the diagnosis of bone abnormalities. Bone irregular and bone discontinuity are two common pathological structures, characterized by abnormal bone surface morphology and structural disruption, respectively. To address the clinical demand for joint detection of these abnormalities, this paper proposes MTL-YOLO, a multitask joint detection model aimed at enhancing automated detection and diagnosis of bone irregularity and discontinuity in medical imaging. Based on the YOLOv8 framework, the proposed method incorporates a multitask attention-guided module, which dynamically adjusts task-relevant regions within shared feature layers, thereby improving the discrimination capability of the model. Additionally, a multitask weighted loss function is designed to balance classification and localization objectives, integrating task-specific losses to further enhance detection accuracy. Experimental results in a self-constructed dataset demonstrate that MTL-YOLO achieves a 4.3 % and 12.3 % improvement in mAP50 and mAP50-95, respectively, over the baseline YOLOv8, with AP gains of 5.1 % and 1.7 % for the detection of bone irregularity and discontinuity. The findings indicate that MTL-YOLO improves joint detection efficiency while maintaining high detection accuracy, providing a viable solution for the automated diagnosis of irregular bone and discontinuity.

title: Teaching Insights on General Physics in Computer Related Professional Courses
authors: Xiaojuan Li, Yachao Wang, Xueya Xue, Yali Wu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: The general physics course based on physics is an important compulsory foundational course for computer related majors, and it occupies a very important position in the computer talent training system. Due to the wide range of knowledge points, broad coverage of knowledge fields and abstract physics concepts in this course, it is difficult for students to master and understand. In addition, there are problems such as outdated teaching methods and single teaching tools in the teaching process of this course, which leads to low student learning enthusiasm and unsatisfactory teaching results. In response to these issues, this article implementes these teaching reform measures, such as AI empowering physics teaching, reforming teaching content and methods, and so on. Through practical exploration, these reform measures have effectively improved students' self-learning and innovation abilities, achieving good teaching results. Meanwhile, this article also summarizes some insights and experiences gained during the teaching process, providing reference and inspiration for future teaching reforms.

title: Efficient Real-Time Object Detection for Indoor Navigation Using Dual YOLO-SSD Networks and MobileNet Backbone
authors: N. Deepa, Manpreet Kaur, Maneesha Singh, Tanmaya Arora, Shivani Sharma, Sneha Kumari, Eshaan Gupta
publish: 2025 8th World Engineering Conference on Contemporary Technologies (WECON)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid deep learning architecture that is designed to improve real-time indoor navigation for the visually impaired. The hybrid model combines two top performing and lightweight object detection architectures: YOLO (You Only Look Once) and SSD (Single Shot Multibox Detector), to achieve better detection precision and responsiveness. A lightweight MobileNet backbone is employed for efficient feature extraction to facilitate deployment on low-resource devices. The results from the YOLO and SSD pathways are combined using Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) to reduce redundant detections and improve final predictions. Experimental results show that the proposed model attains a precision rate of 93%, outperforming YOLOv11, which achieved 89% under comparable conditions. By improving detection accuracy and responsiveness, the proposed model advances the capabilities of assistive navigation systems for visually impaired users. The findings support the capability of hybrid object detection platforms in their construction of smarter, real-time helping technologies.

title: Occlusion-Aware Instance Segmentation for Scene-level Sketches
authors: Chenghao Li, Shiping Dong, Shizhe Zhou
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Sketching is a simple and effective way for humans to express their perception of the world. Sketch instance segmentation plays a crucial role in sketch understanding and is widely used in areas such as sketch recognition, sketchbased image retrieval or editing. Existing image segmentation methods often fail to achieve optimal results due to the modal differences between natural images and sketches, which ignore the sparsity and abstraction of sketches. Existing sketch segmentation methods mainly focus on semantic segmentation of single instance sketches. In this paper, we propose a new maskedregion convolutional neural network based on a large-size firstlayer convolutional kernel for instance segmentation of scenelevel sketches. Our experiments have shown that our method has higher segmentation accuracy for scene level sketches and better segmentation performance for scene level sketches with occlusion.

title: Curriculum Reshaping Supply and Demand: A Comprehensive Approach to Information Technology Curriculum Reform for the Modern Workforce
authors: Weiwei Hu, Fencheng Yang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: This study explores IT curriculum reform in vocational-technical education (VTE) using the Photoshop CS6 (PS CS6) course as a case study. We integrate the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) to align educational outcomes with modern workforce demands, emphasizing competency-based learning and industry relevance. Our approach restructures curricula, implements modern assessment techniques, and leverages the “Tian Jiabing Cup” competition to bridge academia-industry gaps. Results show a 98% student learning efficiency and significant skill enhancement, validating the model's efficacy in preparing graduates for evolving technological landscapes.

title: Empirical Research Status of Generative AI in K-12 Education: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Yutong Liao, Jingjing Zhou, Guangtao Xu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: To investigate the current state of empirical research on generative artificial intelligence in K-12 education, this study employs a systematic literature review methodology to analyze relevant publications from the Web of Science and CNKI databases from January 2022 to June 2025. After screening, 105 valid documents were obtained (11 in Chinese and 94 in English). The study explores three key issues: disciplinary and educational stage distribution, research themes, and application challenges. Research findings indicate that the primary subjects studied include English (15.69%) and mathematics (14.71%), with a focus on junior high school (33%) and senior high school (32%) levels. Research topics can be categorized into four major areas: technology application and effectiveness evaluation, teaching model innovation and competency development, research on learning behaviors and influencing factors, and Exploring Educational Philosophy and Strategies, while challenges such as technical limitations and insufficient research depth persist. This study provides empirical references for the deep integration of technology and basic education.

title: Promoting the Sustainable Development of Intangible Cultural Heritage Education Through Digital Technology Integration: A Case Study of Macao 2049
authors: Yirong Xu, Ziwei Zhou
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: The integration of digital technology and intangible cultural heritage (ICH) is crucial for the sustainable learning of ICH. This study takes the MGM Theatre—Asia's first dynamic high-tech performance venue—as an experimental site to explore the synergy between advanced technological art and the principles of ICH preservation and transmission, with a focus on the interactive stage project Macao 2049. By implementing a model that merges ICH with technology, the project integrates four categories of industrial-grade technical systems: dynamic spatial mapping, human-machine collaborative performance, immersive audiovisual systems, and bionic installation systems. These technologies transform eight forms of Chinese ICH—including Miao folk songs, Yangge dance, Khöömei throat singing, Chakou masks, and Yi narrative singing—from static cultural resources into dynamic knowledge carriers, enabling multi-modal sensory education through a 180-degree panoramic theatre. A betweensubjects user study was conducted with participant groups. The results demonstrate that the platform enhances the efficacy of ICH transmission through a three-stage educational communication mechanism—cultural perception → knowledge internalization → practical application—significantly improving participants' understanding of intangible cultural heritage and positively influencing their learning outcomes. This study highlights the potential of technology-art-ICH integration in promoting sustainable learning behaviors and facilitating effective information delivery, showcasing its educational value as a transformative learning tool that advances the sustainable development of cultural heritage education.

title: The Effects of Simple and Elaborated GAI-Based Feedback on Primary School Students' Programming Performance and Computational Thinking
authors: Yaping Guo, Yi Zhang, Lufang Yu, Dengkang Chen, Ziwen Wei
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Computational thinking (CT) is a core literacy essential for talents in the intelligent era. Although programming learning is widely recognized as an effective means to cultivate CT, its practical impact is limited by the lack of adequate feedback mechanisms. Currently, generative artificial intelligence (GAI) offers new possibilities for improving programming feedback. However, there remains insufficient empirical evidence regarding which type of feedback supported by GAI is more effective. Given this, a six-week quasi-experiment was conducted in a fifth-grade Python programming class. It aimed to investigate whether there were differences in the effects of GAI-based simple feedback (GAI-SF) and GAI-based elaborated feedback (GAI-EF) on students' programming performance, CT, and cognitive load, among other dimensions. The results indicate that, compared to GAI-SF, (1) GAI-EF significantly enhanced students' understanding and internalization of Python knowledge, thereby improving their programming performance; (2) GAI-EF more effectively fostered the development of students' CT, particularly in core dimensions such as decomposition, algorithm design, and assessment; (3) GAI-EF induced a higher cognitive load during its provision. The findings of this study offer valuable insights for instructors, educational technology developers, and researchers planning to integrate GAI into programming instruction. It provides clear practical significance for optimizing the design of GAI-based feedback in programming education.

title: System Protection: Safeguarding Flexible Power System Operations
authors: Daniel Baltensperger, Irina Oleinikova, Kjell Petter Myhren
abstract: The electric power system is essential for modern society, and a malfunction may immediately lead to severe financial damage and, in the worst case, personal injuries. For this reason, extensive measures are taken to ensure the highest possible level of system reliability. Global electricity consumption is expected to grow significantly by 2050 compared to today’s levels. Besides the amount of consumption, the load patterns will be more temperature sensitive and volatile. Furthermore, there is likely to be growth in domestic, selfsufficient, distributed, and clean electricity generation.

title: Universal Reconfigurable Circuit for Interfacing AC and DC Grids
authors: Andrei Blinov, Andrii Chub, Dmitri Vinnikov
publish: 2025 IEEE 66th International Scientific Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering of Riga Technical University (RTUCON)
abstract: The wide adoption of PV panels, batteries, and other natively DC technologies, along with power electronicsinterfaced loads, has instigated research into DC microgrids and associated technologies. To ensure interoperability between consumer and industrial devices and enable compatibility with both AC and DC grids, universal (dualpurpose) converters have been proposed. However, this approach increases redundancy and leads to under-utilization of components, particularly power semiconductor devices, resulting in higher costs and limited feasibility. This paper proposes a reconfigurable input circuit concept that utilizes the same power semiconductors to serve two distinct functions: as a rectifier in AC grids and as solid-state protection in DC grids. In this way, redundancy in universal solutions can be reduced, thereby promoting wider adoption of DC microgrids.

title: Exploring AI-Assisted Homework Completion From the Perspective of English Learners
authors: Qimin Xiao, Ninghua Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: The rapid development of AI tools in the AI era has transformed traditional learning methods, particularly in language learning. An increasing number of English learners are now utilizing AI tools to assist with their homework, raising questions about the current status, learner attitudes, and pedagogical implications of this trend. This study aims to investigate how English learners employ AI tools in completing assignments, their perceptions toward such tools, and the potential pedagogical implicatures. Using a mixed-methods approach involving questionnaire and interview, the research explores the prevalence of AI-assisted homework completion, learners' motivations, and the challenges they encounter. Given the scarcity of similar empirical studies on AI-assisted homework completion, this research contributes to a deeper understanding of AI tool usage among English learners, offering valuable insights for educators and policymakers. The findings may inform pedagogical strategies, curriculum design, and institutional policies to better integrate AI tools in language learning while maintaining academic integrity and learning effectiveness.

title: Ethics of Artificial Intelligence in Education: A Framework for Human-Machine Symbiosis
authors: Xiaoxia Chen, Liguo Zhang, Xiaolin Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: The extensive utilization of robots and generative AI in education presents ethical dilemmas associated with human-machine collaboration. These dilemmas include dependence on technology, the deconstruction of the human subject, and value conflicts between humans and machines. Symbiosis theory suggests that the depth and effectiveness of man-machine symbiosis are influenced by technological innovation, the AI literacy of teachers and students, and the supervision of technology administrators. Different modes of human-machine interaction could either constrain or enhance this symbiotic relationship. Resolving these ethical dilemmas involves promoting complementarity and cognitive alignment between humans and computers, embracing diverse ethics, ongoing monitoring, ensuring mutual benefit, value co-creation, and establishing ethical AI frameworks to support mutual symbiosis in educational ecosystems.

title: Automated Synthesis of Low-Noise Active-RC Filters Considering Op-Amp Effects
authors: Junhan Jia, Yushun Guo
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: This paper presents a highly automated synthesis framework for continuous-time active-RC filters that integrates system-level optimization with circuit-level implementation. By employing generalized matrix-based modeling for topology representation and geometric programming for component optimization, the proposed system achieves a high degree of automation, requiring manual intervention only for the initial Signal Flow Graph (SFG) formulation. The framework features a reverse-derivation algorithm that analytically determines the required Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) from target quality factor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(Q)$</tex> and bandwidth <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(f_{3 d B}\right)$</tex> specifications to compensate for non-ideal pole shifts. Consequently, the system outputs precise GBW specifications, providing critical design targets for the subsequent transistor-level implementation of the Op-Amps. This methodology minimizes manual iteration and optimizes noise performance under strict dynamic range and area constraints, as validated by a second-order filter design example.

title: Proceedings of the International Conference on Digital Economy and Information Technology
authors: N/A
abstract: None

title: Adaptive Learning for IRS-Assisted Wireless Networks: Securing Opportunistic Communications Against Byzantine Eavesdroppers
authors: Amirhossein Taherpour, Abbas Taherpour, Tamer Khattab
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: This paper introduces a unified learning framework for Byzantine-resilient spectrum sensing and secure transmission in intelligent reflecting surface (IRS)-assisted networks under channel state information (CSI) uncertainty. The sensing module employs robust Bayesian belief updates with adversary-resistant aggregation and consensus, guaranteeing reliable primary user (PU) detection even when a bounded fraction of users are malicious. Based on the sensing outcome, the transmission module formulates the downlink design as a sum mean-squared error (MSE) minimization problem under transmit-power and signal-leakage constraints, jointly optimizing the base station (BS) precoder, IRS configuration, and user equalizers. For partial or known CSI, we develop a lightweight alternating optimization algorithm with provable sublinear convergence. For unknown CSI, we integrate constrained Bayesian optimization (BO) within a geometry-aware, low-dimensional latent space. Simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a higher probability of detection at a fixed false-alarm rate under adversarial attacks compared to state-of-the-art schemes. It also yields substantial reductions in user MSE, strong suppression of eavesdropper signal power, and fast convergence. This work provides a practical, resilient solution for coherent sensing–communication coordination in emerging sixth-generation (6G) applications such as vehicular, unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), and Internet of Things (IoT) networks.

title: 2025 5th International Conference on High Performance Computing, Big Data and Communication Engineering (ICHBC)
authors: N/A

title: Intelligent Post-Quantum Cryptography for Secure Consumer Internet of Things Networks
authors: Ishtiaq Ahmad, Liang Yang, Yazeed Alkhrijah, Mohamad A. Alawad, Ahmad Almadhor, Limei Peng
abstract: The rapid growth of consumer Internet of Things (IoT) networks is transforming industries while introducing complex security challenges, particularly in the era of quantum computing, which threatens traditional encryption methods. Post-quantum cryptography (PQC) provides quantum-resistant protection, yet static deployment often fails to meet the dynamic and resource-constrained requirements of IoT systems. To address this, we propose an intelligent and adaptive security framework that integrates lattice-based PQC with a graph neural network (GNN) and a deterministic soft actor-critic (DSAC) algorithm. The GNN continuously monitors network activity to detect anomalies and provides real-time threat intelligence to the DSAC agent, which dynamically adjusts PQC parameters to optimize security. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework can improve security by up to 40% while reducing computational cost and latency by 27% and 46%, respectively, highlighting the potential of AI-driven, post-quantum cryptography for securing dynamic IoT networks.

title: Propensity Formation-Containment Control of Fully Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems via Online Data-Driven Learning
authors: Ao Cao, Fuyong Wang, Zhongxin Liu
abstract: This paper introduces an online data-driven learning scheme designed to address a novel problem in propensity formation and containment control for fully heterogeneous multi-agent systems. Unlike traditional approaches that rely on the eigenvalues of the Laplacian matrix, this problem considers the determination of follower positions based on propensity factors released by leaders. To address the challenge of incomplete utilization of leader information in existing multi-leader control methods, the concept of an influential transit formation leader (ITFL) is introduced. An adaptive observer is developed for the agents, including the ITFL, to estimate the state of the tracking leader or the leader’s formation. Building on these observations, a model-based control protocol is proposed, elucidating the relationship between the regulation equations and control gains, ensuring the asymptotic convergence of the agent’s state. To eliminate the necessity for model information throughout the control process, a new online data-driven learning algorithm is devised for the control protocol. Finally, numerical simulation results are given to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: IEEE Access
authors: N/A

title: A 0.6–0.8 GHz CMOS Frequency-Tunable Nonmagnetic Circulator With Digital Predistortion-Based Linearity Enhancement for In-Band Full-Duplex Radios
authors: Donggu Lee, Chanhee Cho, Taekyu Kim, Seungyeon Kim, Sukju Yun, Seungyoung Park, In-Chul Hwang, Kabseok Ko, Kuduck Kwon
abstract: In this article, a 0.6–0.8 GHz CMOS frequency-tunable nonmagnetic circulator employing a hybrid-capacitor-based C–L–C transmission line and digital-predistortion (DPD)-assisted linearization is presented for advanced in-band full-duplex (IBFD) radios. Each transmission line uses hybrid-capacitors composed of metal-insulator-metal (MIM) capacitors and varactors, providing voltage-controlled tunability and enabling multi-band operation across 5G New Radio (NR) low-band frequencies. An N-path gyrator provides non-reciprocity, while the hybrid-capacitor structure extends the 90° phase-shift bandwidth for adaptive frequency control. To mitigate the intrinsic nonlinearities of the CMOS switches, a DPD algorithm based on the memory-polynomial (MP) model and indirect-learning architecture (ILA) is applied for the first time to a CMOS circulator. Fabricated in a 65-nm CMOS process, the proposed DPD-assisted circulator achieves transmitter and receiver insertion losses of 3.1–3.7 dB and 2.6–3.0 dB, respectively, while maintaining > 30 dB isolation across 600–800 MHz. With a 1024-quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) 5G NR modulation signal (20 MHz bandwidth, peak-to-average power ratio of 11.6 dB) at +10 dBm input power, the DPD improves the error vector magnitude (EVM) from –24.1 dB to –42.8 dB and the adjacent-channel leakage ratio (ACLR) from –26.1 dBc to –44.6 dBc. The circulator consumes 18.1 mW and occupies an active area of 0.34 mm².

title: hGCBF: Heterogeneous Graph Control Barrier Functions for Safe Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Zhibo Yang, Junwei Liu, Zhuohan Li, Wei Zhang
abstract: Provable safety remains a critical challenge for heterogeneous multi-agent systems (MASs), where differences in dynamics, state/action spaces, and sensing capabilities compromise existing learning-based methods that assume system homogeneity. To address this gap, we propose the heterogeneous Graph Control Barrier Function (hGCBF), the first neural control barrier function framework designed for heterogeneous MASs. Our approach introduces a novel extended system formulation via zero-padding to unify heterogeneous state-action spaces, upon which we formally define hGCBF and establish theoretical safety guarantees. We further design a heterogeneous graph neural network architecture with agent-type embeddings and a decoupled node/edge attention mechanism to jointly learn the hGCBF and a distributed controller, enhanced by type-specific loss weighting. Extensive experiments in large-scale 2D and 3D environments demonstrate that our approach achieves over 27% higher safety rates than homogeneous baselines at scale while maintaining strong performance under compositional shifts in agent-type ratios.

title: RFIT2026CFP
authors: N/A

title: Machine Learning-Based EEG Classification for Controlling a Lower-Body Robotic Exoskeleton
authors: Hod Pery, Roi Yozevitch, Eldad Holdengreber
abstract: Brain–computer interface (BCI) research for lower-limb assistance has advanced rapidly; however, many existing studies focus on upper-limb or binary tasks and rely on large multi-user datasets or deep neural models requiring substantial training data. This paper presents a data-efficient EEG-based framework for multi-class lower-body movement recognition and its integration with a robotic exoskeleton. Electroencephalogram (EEG) data were collected using a 14-channel EMOTIV EPOC X headset from 15 participants (ages 23–78, 33% women) performing six periodic lower-body movements: resting stand, resting sit, walking, standing–sitting transition, spin left, and spin right. A single-subject dataset (180 recordings) was used for user-specific calibration, while the complete multi-subject dataset (600 recordings) was used to evaluate robustness under inter-subject variability. Signals were segmented into 2-second windows and processed using a discrete wavelet transform (DWT) with the bior4.4 wavelet at three decomposition levels. Statistical features (energy, entropy, mean, and standard deviation) were classified using a Gradient Boosted Tree (GBT) model. In the single-subject calibration setting, the model achieved 0.742±0.035 accuracy across six classes. In the multi-subject evaluation, DWT-based classification achieved 0.718±0.025, outperforming a Random Forest baseline (0.696±0.022) and a lightweight 1D CNN trained on raw EEG signals (0.549±0.036). Hybrid feature integration combining DWT, band power, and filter bank common spatial patterns further improved multisubject performance to 0.748±0.018, increasing to 0.759±0.020 with 90% overlapping windows. Reduced-class experiments achieved 0.895 ± 0.019 for three movements and 0.936 ± 0.015 for binary classification. The trained classifier was integrated with a six-degree-of-freedom servo-actuated exoskeleton driven by a Hopf oscillator– based central pattern generator (CPG), translating EEG-derived movement states into coordinated actuation commands. These results demonstrate that structured wavelet-based feature extraction combined with ensemble learning provides a computationally efficient and physiologically grounded strategy for adaptive lower-limb neuro-controlled assistive systems.
