The Kappa coefficient of all tested actions exceeds 0.8, and it has significant advantages in matching accuracy, applicability, and generalization ability.

title: Balancing Redundancy Reduction and Feature Retention: a Pca-Svm Approach to Hyperspectral Image Classification
authors: Yang Mengyao, Uzair Aslam Bhatti, Zhang Junfeng, Nodira Sherjanova, Ogabek Sultanov, Yonis Gulzar, Huang Mengxing, Feng Siling
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) has multiple bands that are strongly correlated, which leads to issues such as information redundancy, the curse of dimensionality, and difficulty in data classification. Therefore, to address this, this paper proposes a hyperspectral image classification method that combines Principal Component Analysis (PCA) with Support Vector Machines (SVM). The method first preprocesses the collected hyperspectral images, followed by dimensionality reduction of the preprocessed hyperspectral data using PCA, thereby overcoming issues like information redundancy while retaining the original feature information of the images as much as possible. After reducing the dimensionality of the spectral images, SVM is employed for the final classification and recognition of hyperspectral images. The proposed method is tested on three public datasets: Botswana, Indian Pines and KSC, and the classification accuracy is analyzed under ratios of test sets to training sets. Experimental results show that as the proportion of the training set increases, the OA, AA and Kappa coefficient are significantly improved, and the feature classes become easier to distinguish, which verifies the effectiveness and practicability of the proposed algorithm.

title: FDSCNet: Flexible Dynamic Sparse Compression Network for Music Source Separation
authors: Chengmi Zhang, Dan Guo, Sha Yu
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Music Source Separation (MSS) is vital for applications like audio processing and music production. Traditional MSS methods using fixed-parameter convolutional neural networks struggle with dynamic music signals, limiting separation accuracy. This paper proposes integrating a dynamic convolution mechanism into an SCNet-based spectral-signal separation framework, enhancing music source decomposition with three adaptive kernels. Additionally, a frequency-sensitive hybrid loss function is designed to address limitations of existing loss functions, improving separation performance in key frequency bands by focusing on critical spectral regions. Experiments on the MUSDB18-HQ dataset demonstrate that the proposed FDSCNet achieves superior average SDR performance across vocals, bass, drums, and other sources compared to existing state-of-the-art methods.

title: Reliability Analysis Method of Avionics System Based on Dynamic Bayesian Network
authors: Yukai Hao, Yueyao Niu, Jiao Wu, Tao Wang, Yujie Liang, Yuanhao Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on High Performance Computing, Big Data and Communication Engineering (ICHBC)
abstract: There is a high demand for system reliability in the field of avionics. With the increase in system complexity, it is becoming increasingly difficult to analyze the reliability of integrated avionics systems. This paper summarizes the commonly used reliability analysis methods of avionics systems. Specifically, the dynamic Bayesian network method is used to conduct the reliability analysis of the general integrated modular avionics system architecture. According to the results of reliability and inference analysis, the system architecture design is improved to enhance reliability and reduce system cost. The results indicate that dynamic Bayesian networks can evaluate the reliability of complex avionics systems over time and guide the optimal design of the system.

title: Construction of a Knowledge Graph-Driven Hybrid Teaching Model for C Programming Design
authors: Dan Zhang, Jiaxiang Yao, Dong Zhao, Xiujuan Ma, Jiawei Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: To address the dual challenges of basic skills gap and poor integration of ideological and political education in computer science education in western universities in China, this study proposes an intelligent three-dimensional teaching method for the “C language programming” course, combining knowledge graphs, localized case studies, and adaptive intervention. We use 85 core programming concepts and 16 regional engineering cases from the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau (e.g., ecological data encryption algorithm for Sanjiangyuan protection) combined with a MOOC platform to implement a closed-loop mechanism of “diagnosis-intervention-evaluation”. This study builds a replicable framework for the integration of technology mastery and value cultivation in engineering education.

title: Smoothed Markov Chains for MITRE ATT&CK Prediction: Addressing Data Scarcity in Cyber Kill Chains
authors: Karthik Pappu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: The MITRE ATT&CK framework is a standard tool to characterize adversary behavior, but forecasting how attackers will progress based on observed tactics remains a challenging problem. Tactic sequences derived from cyber threat intelligence (CTI) provide incomplete and sparsely sampled representations of adversary activity, leaving many transitions unobserved and complicating probabilistic modeling. To address this, we apply a smoothed Markov Chain model that uses interpolated absolute discounting to mitigate zeroprobability issues caused by data sparsity. Our method reallocates probability mass from frequently observed transitions to unseen transitions while maintaining interpretability. We evaluated our approach on a dataset of 682 ATT&CK tactic sequences constructed from CTI reports, MITRE Attack Flow artifacts, and adversary emulation plans. The results are reported as mean ± standard deviation over five randomized train/test splits. Our experiments show that a standard Markov model attains the highest Top-5 accuracy (84.1% ± 0.7%), while the smoothed model improves Macro-F1 from 5.9% to 15.5% ± 4.1%. This improvement is statistically significant (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.01$</tex>, paired t-test). Under conditions of limited data, smoothed Markov models provide an interpretable approach for predicting attack progression in cyber threat intelligence.

title: ProtoDecNet: A Prototype-Based Representation Decoupling Network for Multimodal Cancer Survival Prediction
authors: Xinyu Li, Jin Song, Yizhong Zhang, Tong Tong
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Accurate cancer survival prediction is essential for prognosis assessment and clinical decision-making. Integrating histopathological whole-slide images (WSIs) and genomic profiles has shown promising results; however, multimodal survival analysis remains challenging due to modality heterogeneity, feature sparsity, and redundant cross-modal representations. We propose ProtoDecNet, a prototype-based representation decoupling network for multimodal cancer survival prediction. ProtoDecNet learns compact modality-specific and cross-modal prototype representations to effectively capture complementary representations while mitigating redundancy. An Adaptive Token Enhancement Module (ATEM) is introduced to enrich unimodal prototypes by emphasizing informative features, while a Common Representation Encoder (CRE) is designed to decouple shared cross-modal representations in a lightweight and robust manner. By operating at the prototype level, ProtoDecNet enables efficient modeling of large-scale WSIs and high-dimensional genomic data. Empirical evaluations across three TCGA cohorts show that ProtoDecNet consistently surpasses existing unimodal and multimodal approaches. Ablation studies and risk stratification analyses further confirm the effectiveness and clinical relevance of the proposed framework.

title: The Educator as an Embedded AI Designer: Faculty Agency in Generative AI-Enhanced Pedagogy
authors: Liu Liu, Ng Sook Mun, Adi Suryanata Herwana, Markus Yeo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is rapidly reshaping higher education, yet existing research remains largely focused on student use and institutional governance. The role of faculty as active designers of AI-enhanced pedagogy rather than passive adopters remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce the Embedded AI Designer Framework, where educators serve as co-designers who incorporate disciplinary reasoning, ethical norms, and pedagogical intent into GenAI systems, instead of acting as passive users of AI tools. We describe how this framework was applied across three disciplines at the National University of Singapore. These implementations demonstrate how faculty agency is exercised in three stages: Intentional Design, Orchestrated Mediation, and Reflective Alignment. Rather than replacing instructors, the use of GenAI allows them to exercise professional judgment, transforming AI from a mere automation tool into a medium of pedagogical intentionality. In other words, we propose a pedagogy-first paradigm in employing AI in higher education, where human expertise and design literacy drive responsible and meaningful integration.

title: Digital-Twin-driven multi-hop collaborative offloading for industrial monitoring
authors: Lingling Liao, Ming Tao, Junkang Cai, Renping Xie, Xiaokai Cao, Qunjian Chen, Ke Mu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on High Performance Computing, Big Data and Communication Engineering (ICHBC)
abstract: The proliferation of sensing terminals in industrial monitoring environments has led to a significant increase in computational tasks, imposing stringent demands on both computing power and low-latency. With the limited resources of terminals, the mismatch between demands and resource constraints constitutes a key bottleneck to improve monitoring efficiency. Although resource pressure is alleviated by edge computing through the end-edge direct offloading mode, the significant spatial imbalance in monitoring tasks often leads to issues. To fully unleash the potential of distributed computing resources, when a direct edge server lacks sufficient resources, permitting tasks to be forwarded via a multi-hop mechanism to more suitable nodes, is a promising solution. To this end, a digital-twin (DT)-driven edge computing power network framework for industrial monitoring is constructed. On the basis of clarifying the models design, with the objective of minimizing the average task processing delay, the multi-hop collaborative offloading is formulated as an optimization problem. Leveraging the DT environment as a reliable platform for training and validation, a proximal policy optimization (PPO)-based offloading algorithm combined with a cosine annealing learning rate scheduler, dubbed PPOCALR, is designed, autonomously learning to generate optimal offloading decisions, and achieving low-latency and highly reliable monitoring services. Simulation results demonstrate that PPOCALR outperforms compared algorithms in key performance metrics, validating the effectiveness and superiority.

title: Research on VETS Certificate Quality Assurance Mechanism in the Era of Artificial Intelligence
authors: Xiao Xiao, Fengyun Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: In the era of artificial intelligence, Vocational English Test System (VETS) certificate pilot quality confronts many challenges. Based on 173 survey subjects data, this study investigates the current situation of VETS certificate in different vocational colleges, in order to explore reasons from policy support, teaching practice and enterprise needs. Policy recommendations include co-constructing base, training teacher, co-developing course, integrating competition and certificate and improving scientific research competence.

title: Perturbation-Based Pinning Control Strategy for Enhanced Synchronization in Complex Networks
authors: Ziang Mao, Tianlong Fan, Linyuan Lü
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: Synchronization is essential for the stability and coordinated operation of complex networked systems. Pinning control, which selectively controls a subset of nodes, provides a scalable solution to enhance network synchronizability. However, existing strategies face key limitations. Heuristic centrality-based methods lack a direct connection to synchronization dynamics, while spectral approaches, though effective, are computationally intensive. To address these challenges, we propose a perturbation-based optimized (PBO) strategy that dynamically evaluates each node’s spectral impact on the Laplacian matrix, achieving improved synchronizability with significantly reduced computational costs (with complexity <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(kM)$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional strategies in synchronizability, convergence rate, and pinning robustness to node failures. Notably, in all the empirical networks tested and some generated networks, PBO significantly outperforms the brute-force greedy (BFG) strategy, demonstrating its ability to avoid local optima and adapt to complex connectivity patterns. Our study establishes the theoretical relationship between network synchronizability and convergence rate, offering new insights into efficient synchronization strategies for large-scale complex networks.

title: From COCOMO to GPT: A Comprehensive Evaluation of LLM-Based Software Effort Estimation
authors: Feisal Alaswad, E Poovammal, Batoul Aljaddouh
abstract: Accurate software effort estimation remains a critical yet challenging task in software engineering. While recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across a wide range of software engineering activities, their effectiveness for effort estimation has not been systematically evaluated. This paper presents a comprehensive empirical benchmark comparing zero-shot LLM-based estimation with classical Machine Learning (ML) models and transformer-based regression approaches across two complementary settings: textual story-point estimation and structured project-level effort estimation. We evaluate multiple model families on two large-scale story-point datasets (Agile and NEO) and five widely adopted project-level datasets (Desharnais, COCOMO81, NASA93, Maxwell, and Kitchenham). Predictive accuracy is assessed across all datasets. The story-point benchmark further analyzes cross-project generalization, linguistic robustness, computational cost, and human–AI interaction effects, whereas the project-level benchmark evaluates robustness under noise injection, missing-value stress testing, and feature ablation. Experimental results show that off-the-shelf LLMs operating in zero-shot settings consistently underperform task-specific ML and transformer-based regression models in terms of absolute accuracy and cost efficiency. ClassicalMLmodels remain the most reliable estimators for structured datasets, while fine-tuned transformer regressors achieve the best performance on textual story-point estimation. Despite their lower numerical accuracy, LLMs demonstrate relatively low sensitivity to linguistic variation and may influence human judgment through persuasive explanations, as suggested by the exploratory human–LLM interaction study. These findings suggest that zero-shot LLMs may be suited as qualitative decision-support tools rather than as autonomous effort estimators. Overall, this work highlights the importance of architectural alignment, cost-aware evaluation, and human-centered considerations when deploying AI models for software effort estimation.

title: Decentralized Traffic Right-of-way Negotiation: A Blockchain-sequenced consensus framework for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Cheng Li, Hao Wu, Lei Xiong, Yunlong Lu, Chen Dong, Huiping Liu, Yue Liu
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) connects roadside units, vehicles, and pedestrians into an efficient communication network. However, ensuring honesty among vehicles in such a decentralized system remains a challenge, especially for intersection management. To address this issue, we propose a blockchain-based IoV credit system. Drivers’ rushing behavior is a primary contributor to accidents at intersections. To solve this problem and enhance traffic efficiency, we propose a strategy that allows time-sensitive convoys to pass through an intersection ahead of others by paying traffic tokens via IoV while still complying with traffic regulations. To protect the privacy of the yielding vehicles, we develop an optimal pricing strategy for the rushing convoys using a dynamic game model with incomplete information, which is solved using a refined Bayesian Nash equilibrium. Furthermore, we introduce a blockchain consensus mechanism that employs a sequence chain to ensure a unique and consistent order for passing. Both theoretical analysis and simulations show that the proposed strategy is feasible and effective in meeting the practical need for low latency at intersections. It increases the likelihood of successful transactions, protects yielding convoys from lower benefits, and encourages broader participation.

title: GRKAN: Gate Recurrent Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks for UUV Noncooperative Target Trajectory Tracking
authors: Dan Yu, Changjian Lin
abstract: Noncooperative underwater target trajectory tracking is essential for enabling uncrewed underwater vehicles (UUV) to perform precise guidance, collision avoidance, and target capture tasks. Traditional Bayesian methods, which rely on predefined predictive and measurement models, have demonstrated limited effectiveness due to inherent uncertainty in underwater sonar observations and the unpredictable dynamics of noncooperative targets. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel approach to developing gate recurrent Kolmogorov–Arnold networks (GRKAN) for UUV noncooperative target trajectory tracking. Unlike conventional methods that require explicit target dynamics and sonar observation parameters to construct predictive models, GRKAN reformulates tracking as sequence-based learning. This approach learns patterns of target motion and sonar observations from historical data to build a predictive model for noncooperative target trajectories. The GRKAN incorporates two key innovations: first, a recurrent Kolmogorov–Arnold representation for maintaining short-term memory of system states, and second, gating mechanisms for efficient information flow management. Furthermore, by integrating the Monte Carlo principle with GRKAN, we develop a sampling strategy within the observation space to address the uncertainty of posterior distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that the GRKAN-based approach significantly improves the accuracy and stability of noncooperative target trajectory tracking, particularly under conditions of uncertain observation.

title: Spatial-Temporal High-Frequency Learning for Video-based Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
authors: Sichen Tao, Shuang Li, Jun Ye, Neng Dong, Fan Li, Huafeng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Video-based Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification (VVI-ReID) aims to learn consistent person feature representations across video sequences in different modalities. Existing methods that use an intermediate modality to bridge the gap between visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) sequences tend to be limited by high construction costs, loss of high-frequency details, and lack of temporal cues. Moreover, they typically focus on refining global representation using high-level features, neglecting the enhancement of local details through low-level features. To address these challenges, we propose the novel Spatial-Temporal High-Frequency Learning (STHF) framework, which constructs an appropriate intermediate modality for the VVI-ReID task and alleviates the modality gap via hierarchical feature enhancement. Specifically, we introduce the Spatial-Temporal High-Pass Filter (ST-HPF), which filters out spatial-temporal Low-Frequency Components (LFC), preserving high-frequency details to construct an intermediate modality at the sequence level. We then enhance the local details with low-level features through the Shallow Detail Compensation (SDC) module, which reduces local noise interference. Finally, the Deep Semantic Refinement (DSR) module refines the global representation by modeling spatial-temporal high-frequency semantic associations using high-level features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on the publicly available HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus datasets. The code is available at https://github.com/TSC95720/STHF.

title: Versatile Modeling with FDTD of Maxwell’s and LLG Equations
authors: Li Li, Yuanxun Ethan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: A new versatile modeling method has been proposed for simulations of three-dimensional (3-D) arbitrarily shaped RF magnetic components. The simulations are accomplished through the hybrid computations of electrodynamics and micromagnetics, where the Maxwell’s equations and Landau-Lifshitz Gilbert (LLG) equation are jointly solved. The method solves the Maxwell-LLG system based on the mesh cells of fixed material distributions in space. The field variables are computed with the FDTD method through the leap-frog-processing (LFP). In the modeling, a Yee cell implementation allows the boundary conditions on the material surfaces to be satisfied with the Maxwell equations coupled with the LLG equation effectively. The implementation also utilizes a material mapping (MM) solution to determine the material property of each mesh element generated by the ray-casting method (RCM). Moreover, a double damping perfect matching layer (PML) technique is implemented as the absorbing boundary condition of the simulations, where the ferromagnetic material can extend into the PML regions. The parallel programming technique is utilized for the FDTD-LFP to improve the computational efficiency. The numerical solution has proven to be effective and stable even for simulations of RF devices in resonances with low loss ferromagnetic materials.

title: Contraction-Relaxation Behavior Inspired Fractional-Order Control for Physical Human-Robot Interaction with Optimized Order
authors: Junhui Liu, Zhiqiang Ma, Hanlin Dong, Panfeng Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper proposes a fractional-order control method for the physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) inspired by the dynamics of muscle contraction-relaxation behavior, which is integrated into a fractional-order sliding mode with variable order and parameters, to achieve fast transient response while guaranteeing high-precision steady-state tracking performance. A sensorless force observer based on fractional calculus is developed to estimate the operator’s behavior, enabling a composite control system that guarantees ultimate boundedness of the closed-loop signals. The deep reinforcement learning-based order and parameter optimization mechanism is synthesized into the unified architecture of the composite control system. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated through numerical simulations and comparative studies, which demonstrate significant improvements in convergence speed on the sliding manifold while maintaining steady-state precision. Experimental results further confirm the feasibility and practical applicability of the framework in a cylindrical docking scenario via visualreality fusion approach, highlighting its potential for future semiautonomous human-in-loop missions.

title: Estimating impurities impact in seasonal snow albedo with remote sensing during a wildfire event in Bariloche, Argentina
authors: Giuliana Beltramone, Marcelo C. Scavuzzo, Francisco Nemiña, Matias Bonansea, María José Esplandiu, Anabella Ferral
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: This study investigates the impact of light-absorbing impurities (LAIs) from wildfire ashes on seasonal snow albedo in the Patagonian Andes near San Carlos de Bariloche, Argentina. A multi-faceted approach was applied, integrating satellite remote sensing, radiative transfer simulations, and in situ measurements to assess albedo reduction and spectral modifications. The methods employed include calculating percentage variation, visible range slope, normalized indices, and polynomial coefficients to quantify albedo change and impurities’ effects on reflectance. The results show a marked drop in albedo after the November 2, 2022, wildfire, particularly in visible and near infrared bands, reflecting the deposition of ashes and carbonaceous particles on the snow surface. The combination of multi-source data and methods underscores the utility of satellite observation to track and quantify the effects of such events on snow-covered regions.

title: Research on Personalized Learning Path Recommendations for Physics Based on Generative AI: Using the “Smart and Fun Physics Assistant” as a Platform
authors: Siyuan Huang, Zijian He
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Generative AI is reshaping the educational ecosystem, with UNESCO and relevant Chinese authorities promoting its safe application in education. This study explores the use of generative AI in personalized physics learning through the “Smart and Fun Physics Assistant” platform. It addresses four core challenges: enhancing comprehension of abstract physics principles via dynamic visualization; generating personalized learning paths based on student data; supplementing limited experimental conditions with virtual experiments; and enabling AI teaching assistants for learning analytics and differentiated instruction. The system employs adaptive learning theory and generative AI technology to construct a three-tier architecture supported by user interaction and data. Using projectile motion instruction as a case study, it demonstrates the system's ability to deepen understanding and reduce teacher workload. Innovations include adjustable-parameter experiment animations and personalized learning paths, though limitations such as insufficient voice interaction remain. This research provides a paradigm for integrating generative AI with physics education, advancing educational digital transformation.

title: Performance Analysis of Virtual Machine Migration Strategy in Cloud Computing Environment
authors: Lu Zhenfeng
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: In view of the challenges of virtual machine migration in cloud computing environment, this study proposes an efficient migration strategy to optimize resource allocation and improve service quality. Based on the theory of resource scheduling, load balancing algorithm and performance optimization model and strategy by building a dependency graph and intelligent scheduling algorithm, combined with incremental synchronization technology down time reduced by 30%. Using dynamic resource adapter and the hardware abstraction layer optimization, supporting heterogeneous hardware environment seamless migration, improve resource utilization rate of 30%. The compression algorithm and encryption technology are integrated to reduce network bandwidth consumption by 20%. Experimental validation in the hybrid cloud environments, the results show that the strategy to improve system stability by 25% increase to 25%, the response speed and is suitable for accounted for 40% of the hybrid cloud deployment scenarios. Study provides the reliable technology for cloud computing resources dynamic optimization scheme, power industry high quality development.

title: Modelling and Comparison of Common Mode Conducted Emissions of Current-Fed Partial Power Converter for Different Modulation Schemes
authors: Hetal Sharma, Andrii Chub, Lauri Kütt, Neelesh Yadav
publish: 2025 IEEE 66th International Scientific Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering of Riga Technical University (RTUCON)
abstract: Partial Power Converters (PPCs) offer an efficient solution for integrating battery energy storage systems with DC microgrids. However, the high-frequency switching in PPCs can potentially introduce common mode (CM) noise, which can compromise microgrid stability. This study focuses on understanding CM noise generation and propagation mechanisms in a PPC topology based on current-fed dual active bridge converter proposed for such applications. An equivalent CM circuit model is developed and further utilized to analyse CM noise behaviour under two operating modes with different modulation schemes - phase-shift and flyback modulation. The model enables the derivation of analytical expressions that support a comparative evaluation of CM noise characteristics across these modes. LTspice simulations are conducted to validate the modelling approach and analytical results.

title: Background-Divergence-Adaptive Spectral-Constrained Autoencoder for Hyperspectral Anomaly Change Detection
authors: Houwei Liu, Chenxi Guo, Xing Hu, Jie Kong
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Hyperspectral anomaly change detection aims to identify subtle changes in multi-temporal hyperspectral images and demands high detection accuracy. However, hyperspectral images typically contain hundreds of spectral bands, and their complex spectral features often cause significant interference to detection results. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Background-Divergence-Adaptive Spectral-Constrained Autoencoder (BDAS-AE) model. By incorporating an adaptive spectral-angle constraint into the conventional mean squared error loss function, the model effectively enhances the background modeling capability of hyperspectral images. Additionally, an innovative method for measuring the divergence between multi-temporal hyperspectral images is introduced to automatically adjust the weight of the spectral-angle constraint. Experimental results on the publicly available “Viareggio 2013” dataset demonstrate that the proposed BDAS-AE significantly outperforms existing methods in detection performance.

title: Research on Cross-Domain Attack Defense and Endogenous Security Mechanism in Power Dispatching Systems Based on Microservices
authors: Xin Xu, Gang Qu, Liqun Yang, Renhui Dou, Zeliang Shen, Zhiqiang Yao
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: As power dispatching systems evolve toward microservice architectures, containerized modules for scheduling, trading, data collection, and auditing enhance flexibility but also expand the attack surface, exposing risks such as link hijacking, container escape, and cross-domain privilege escalation. This paper presents an endogenous security framework that integrates SM2/SM4-based encrypted communication, zero-trust access control, and runtime anomaly detection. Mutual authentication ensures secure links, while OPA-UEBA enforces fine-grained authorization, and eBPF with IsolationForest enables real-time threat detection. Experiments demonstrate over 99% link integrity, 97% node trust, 98% access accuracy, and sub- 150 ms latency, offering a practical model for resilient power dispatching security.

title: Improved YOLOv8n-based Algorithm for Road Object Detection in Foggy Weather
authors: Wenjie Huang, Zhenyu Chen, Dan Zhang, Fengmin Peng, Mingshen Huang, Guojin Xiao
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Due to the limited feature representation capability of the YOLOv8n model under complex weather conditions leading to decreased detection accuracy, this paper proposes a novel Pyramid Pooling Attention with Low-rank Convolution (PPA_LC) module based on YOLOv8n. Specifically, we first designed an attention mechanism based on multi-scale pooling, which captures cross-regional information through a hierarchical fusion strategy. This attention mechanism enhances feature representation capability and the ability to capture blurred contour features while only adding 0.018 M parameters. Additionally, “PPA_LC” adopts the concept of low-rank decomposition to reconstruct standard <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 \times 7$</tex> convolution into <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 7$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7 \times 1$</tex> spatially separable convolutions. This maintains a large receptive field while significantly reducing parameters. The PPA_LC module employs an embedded design that can seamlessly replace the C2f module in the YOLOv8n structure while maintaining the original network's computational cost (GFLOPs increased by only 0.8% compared to the original YOLOv8n model). Results show that the overall accuracy reaches 88.3% (mAP50) and 70.8% (mAP50-95) under two performance evaluation metrics, showing improvement compared to the unmodified YOLOv8n.

title: Sound Event Detection Based on M-LFCC Feature Fusion
authors: Chao Zhang, Yu Zhao, Yin Cao, Yi Zhou, Kebing Qing, Yurong Wu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Sound Event Detection (SED) is crucial for intelligent perception systems. Robust feature representation is fundamental to SED performance. Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) feature is widely used but it lacks high-frequency details, and thus limits its performance in applications that rely on detecting high-frequency sounds, such as security monitoring or industrial fault diagnosis. This paper proposes an improved model to address this limitation. The model integrates MFCC with Linear-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (LFCC). LFCC provides better high-frequency resolution. This fusion offers a more comprehensive spectral representation. The fused features are then processed by an Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture contextual temporal dependencies. The output of the LSTM is subsequently fed into an Emphasized Channel Attention, Propagation and Aggregation in Time Delay Neural Network (ECAPA-TDNN) backbone, where the Efficient Channel Attention module replaces the original Squeeze-and-Excitation module to enhance cross-channel interaction while reducing model complexity. It also enhances cross-channel interaction. Experiments are conducted on the ESC-50 dataset. The proposed model is compared with three state-of-the-art methods. The results show its superior performance with a classification accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 5. 1 3 \%}$</tex>, which outperforms the compared methods. It validates the effectiveness of the MFCC-LFCC fusion strategy and the improved architecture for SED tasks.

title: Resilience or Reliability: Which Matters Most? [Editor’s Voice]
authors: Innocent Kamwa, Julia Matevosyan

title: Towards a Hybrid Human-Machine Framework for Gunshot Identification and Police Notification in Jamaica
authors: Chavaree Campbell, Julian Jarrett, Kimberley Hemmings-Jarrett
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Collaboration and Internet Computing (CIC)
abstract: As crime rates rise and citizens become increasingly reluctant to report incidents due to fear of retaliation, there is growing demand for technological interventions. This need is especially urgent in developing countries like Jamaica, where public safety directly impacts tourism and economic stability. In response, this study proposes a human-in-the-loop framework that leverages the strengths of both human and machine capabilities in gunshot detection and reporting. Using a sample of Jamaican citizens, the study compares the accuracy of human versus machine classification of gunshot sounds and examines citizens’ willingness to report such incidents. The proposed framework addresses technological limitations such as miscalculation, as well as social barriers like fear and distrust, offering a balanced solution for environments where a single strategy may be ineffective or unavailable.

title: Cache-Optimized Shared Embedding Space For Seamless Cross-Modal Search
authors: G Uday Kiran, V Srilakshmi, G Padmini, K Sri Snigdha, K Siva Praneetha
publish: 2025 8th World Engineering Conference on Contemporary Technologies (WECON)
abstract: Cross-modal retrieval, which allows for information search between multiple data modalities like images and text, is becoming ever more important in handling large-scale multimodal content. Conventional systems sometimes struggle to efficiently align semantic meaning between modalities. This paper introduces an efficient cross-modal retrieval framework that blends Contrastive Language Image Pre-training (CLIP) and the FAISS similarity search library, augmented further by the novel Cache Augmented Generation (CAG) mechanism. CLIP is employed to embed both visual and text inputs into a common embedding space for unified bidirectional retrieval, image-to-text, and text-to-image, without distinct pipelines. FAISS indexing provides efficient approximate nearest-neighbor search capability, and CAG adds a dynamic cache that caches frequent query-response pairs and intermediate embeddings to avoid redundant computation and latency enhancements. The caching approach also facilitates partial reuse of embeddings under similar query patterns, further improving real-time responsiveness. Evaluation on Flickr30k and Conceptual Captions datasets shows better retrieval quality with less latency, confirming its potential for real-time, cross-modal search use cases. Beyond these benchmarks, the proposed framework has broader applicability to domains such as healthcare imaging, e-commerce product recommendation, and digital libraries, where semantic precision and real-time responsiveness are critical. Furthermore, its cache-optimized design highlights potential for deployment in large-scale, streaming, and multilingual environments, paving the way for next-generation multimodal search systems.

title: Test Requirement Modeling and Verification Method Oriented Towards Intelligent Characteristics of Autonomous Unmanned Equipment
authors: Jie You, Jiayu Tong, Tong Yang, Hongbin Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: Addressing the challenges of state space explosion and the lack of test oracles in testing the intelligent characteristics of autonomous unmanned equipment, this paper proposes a fullprocess methodology for test requirement modeling and verification. The approach first utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) technology to construct a requirement graph, achieving formal modeling through a dual-view approach involving State Machines (STM) and Binary Decision Diagrams (BDD). Subsequently, an intelligent test case generation strategy is designed by integrating Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) with Metamorphic Testing, followed by reduction using Pearson correlation coefficients. Finally, closed-loop verification is implemented on a simulation platform based on the Functional Mock-up Unit (FMU) standard and Lua scripting. Experimental results demonstrate that this method effectively covers intelligent decision-making paths and significantly enhances the completeness and efficiency of testing.

title: Data-Driven Leakage Power Prediction and Optimization for Analog Circuits Via Machine Learning and Deep Neural Networks
authors: Ziyang Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: This paper presents a data-driven framework for predicting and reducing leakage power in analog integrated circuits. As technology scales down, leakage has become a dominant factor in total power consumption, making accurate estimation essential for energy-efficient design. To address this, a comprehensive feature dataset for the C499 benchmark circuit is generated through transistor-level NGSpice simulations across various process, voltage, and temperature conditions. Multiple machine learning and deep learning models are trained to capture the nonlinear relationships between circuit parameters and leakage power. Model performance is evaluated using MAPE and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2}$</tex> metrics, with deep neural networks achieving the best accuracy and generalization. Feature importance analysis further identifies key factors such as device geometry, bias voltage, and tunneling current as major contributors to leakage. Based on these insights, model-guided design adjustments are proposed, demonstrating that combining NGSpice simulation data with ML/DL techniques enables accurate leakage prediction and effective power optimization for analog circuits.

title: Deep learning reveals spatial patterns in water contamination over Ciénaga de la Virgen using Sentinel-2 imagery.
authors: Yineth Viviana Camacho-De Angulo, Jairo Enrique Serrano-Castañeda, Yady Tatiana Solano-Correa
publish: 2025 Latin American GRSS &amp; ISPRS Remote Sensing Conference (LAGIRS)
abstract: Water pollution in the Bolívar department of Colombia is a critical environmental problem. This problem affects aquatic ecosystems, water quality, and access to clean water for domestic, agricultural, and industrial use. Conventional water contamination monitoring methods are often costly, laborious, and limited in spatial and temporal coverage. This makes accurate and timely assessments, particularly in large or difficult to access regions. Moreover, the dependence on spot sampling and laboratory analysis limits the availability of the results. This limits the ability to respond to unexpected contamination events. This limits the ability to respond to unexpected contamination events. These limitations have prompted the adoption of alternative approaches. These include the use of real-time sensors, remote sensing, and satellite imagery. Predictive models based on artificial intelligence have also been developed. These tools allow optimizing water body monitoring and extend the spatial and temporal coverage of water contamination monitoring. In this context, this study aims to address the challenges of conventional monitoring. This study proposes a deep learning-based model to estimate and predict contamination in strategic wetlands, with a particular focus on Ciénaga de la Virgen. The methodology integrates Sentinel-2 remote sensing data with in-situ measurements of key indicators of water contamination. A dataset was constructed by combining spectral information and water contamination parameters from monitoring stations. The results achieved a promising prediction of the contamination variables. The proposed framework facilitates sustainable management of water resources and contributes to understanding the dynamics of contamination.

title: LiteCNN: A Lightweight Model for Face Recognition in Mask-Occluded Scenarios
authors: Qi Zhang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has led to a global health crisis, making mask-wearing in public areas an essential protective measure. However, the presence of face masks significantly degrades the performance of traditional face recognition systems that rely on full face features. Most existing studies predominantly utilize Western face datasets and employ models with high computational complexity, which hinders deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. To address these issues, this paper introduces an Asian Masked Face Dataset (AMFD), which contains <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 0, 0 0 0}$</tex> images and includes ten-class dataset, and proposes LiteCNN, a lightweight seven-layer Convolutional Neural Network. The model incorporates batch normalization and dropout mechanisms, achieving high accuracy while substantially reducing computational complexity. Experimental results demonstrate that the LiteCNN achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8. 1 \%}$</tex> recognition accuracy on the AMFD, moreover, training on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 GPU takes only 23 minutes, outperforming other popular lightweight models. This work provides an efficient solution for real-time masked face recognition on lightweight offline devices.

title: 3D Printed Ultra-Wideband and Ultrathin Absorber Leveraging the Synergy of Copper Paste Metasurface and Magnetic Medium
authors: Yutong Gao, Chuwen Lan, Guoan Zhao, Zehua Gao
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: In this study, an ultrathin, ultra-wideband, and easily preparable absorber with operating frequencies ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$K u$</tex> bands was designed and experimentally verified. The absorber is composed of a bilayer copper paste metasurface and an ultrathin magnetic medium. The magnetic medium can reduce the overall thickness while expanding the absorption bandwidth to both low and high frequencies. A novel fabrication technique combining 3D printing and spray coating processes is introduced, with which planar and irregular absorbers are easily prepared. Experimental results reveal more than 90% absorption within 2.8–18 GHz, with a thickness of only 0.04 times the maximum wavelengths.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization Model and Enhanced Genetic Algorithm for Fresh Food E-Commerce Distribution under Time Window Constraints
authors: Fugui Fang, Jian Yang
publish: 2025 IEEE 8th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Artificial Intelligence (PRAI)
abstract: Driven by increasing consumer demand for fresh food products and elevated expectations for fast, high-quality delivery, efficient logistics systems have become a critical challenge for the fresh food e-commerce industry. This study focuses on the Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (VRPTW) in the context of fresh food ecommerce distribution. A multi-objective optimization model is developed to simultaneously minimize total costs, including distribution expenses, fixed vehicle costs, penalties for time window violations, and costs related to product freshness deterioration. The key constraints considered are vehicle capacity limits and customer-specific delivery time windows. To address this complex logistical problem, an enhanced Genetic Algorithm (GA) integrated with an elite preservation strategy is proposed. Simulation experiments validate the effectiveness of the model and algorithm. The results show that the optimized solution achieves a substantial 19.18 % reduction in total distribution costs compared to baseline scenarios. The proposed approach demonstrates strong performance and practical applicability, offering a promising solution for improving logistics efficiency in the fresh food e-commerce sector.

title: An Empirical Study on the Impact of Generative Artificial Intelligence-Assisted Programming on Computational Thinking in Secondary School Students
authors: Rong Guo, Haifei Miao, Guian Li, Zhenzhong Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence has demonstrated remarkable potential in the field of education due to its powerful text-based capabilities. However, its impact on programming education remains in the early exploratory phase, with limited empirical research available. This study involved 83 high school students to investigate the effects of generative AI-assisted programming on their computational thinking skills. A mixed-methods approach was adopted, incorporating tests and semi-structured interviews to collect both quantitative and qualitative data. The results indicate that generative AI-assisted programming significantly enhances students' computational thinking skills. Furthermore, this learning strategy promotes personalized learning of programming. The findings of this study can be applied in practical programming education to improve students' computational thinking.

title: Simultaneous Water-Filling with Fractional Frequency Reuse
authors: Jiawen Sun
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: In the transition from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 G}$</tex>, traditional wireless communication simulation frameworks face challenges such as low efficiency, oversimplified models, and significant deviations from real-world performance. To address these issues, this paper proposes an optimized framework that combines Fractional Frequency Reuse (FFR) and Simultaneous Water Filling (SWF). Scenario authenticity is improved by introducing log-normal shadow fading (8 dB in urban areas and 4 dB in rural areas) and a random walk mobility model (step length 0.1). Algorithm efficiency is enhanced through parallel computing in MATLAB, as well as optimized software initialization and convergence strategies. Simulation results demonstrate smoother capacity curves and more realistic blocking probabilities (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 \%-60 \%$</tex>) under high SNR, with urban scenarios showing better performance due to the dense deployment of base stations. The framework provides a reliable tool for resource allocation studies in emerging 6 G applications such as intelligent manufacturing and autonomous driving. However, limitations remain, including simplified mobility models, a lack of full-band 6 G channels, and insufficient support for distributed frameworks. Future work will focus on refined scenario modeling, intelligent collaborative algorithms, engineering-based validation, and integration with real-world networks to support scalable and practical 6 G deployment.

title: A CiteSpace-Based Visualization Analysis of Research Hotspots and Evolutionary Trends in Educational Robotics
authors: Rui Fan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence and robotics into education has established educational robotics as a critical component of intelligent learning environments. However, the growing body of literature and inherent interdisciplinary complexity have made it increasingly challenging to grasp the field's knowledge structure and evolutionary dynamics. This study conducts a comprehensive bibliometric analysis to map research hotspots, trace developmental pathways, and identify emerging trends within educational robotics. Using CiteSpace v6.4.R1, we examined 281 relevant articles from the Web of Science Core Collection published between January 2012 and August 2025. Through keyword co-occurrence analysis, burst detection, and timezone visualization, three major research themes were identified: computational thinking and STEM skill development, robotics in elementary and childhood education, and educational robot design and technological integration. The evolution of the field was divided into three distinct phases: foundational exploration, pedagogical expansion, and specialization and efficacy. Current research frontiers include AI-robot integration, the evolving role of educators in human - AI collaborative settings, longitudinal impact assessments, and ethical implementation challenges. The study provides a systematic overview of the intellectual landscape of educational robotics, offering valuable insights for future research and practice aimed at fostering technological and pedagogical advancement in the field.

title: An Intelligent Fault Identification Model for Distribution Networks Based on Deep Learning
authors: Qiang Wei, Wenlong Xu, Wenlong Zhu, Guangyu Gao, Weijian Xu, Jingpeng Tan
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Electronic Information Engineering and Computer Communication (EIECC)
abstract: With the continuous advancement of power system technology and the upgrading of equipment, the accumulation of power operation data has increasingly shown regular characteristics. However, traditional neural networks, limited by their own architecture and performance, struggle to achieve ideal results in fault sample identification tasks. To address this issue, this paper proposes an intelligent fault identification model for distribution networks based on Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Firstly, the principle and advantages of the GRU network as the core architecture of the model were elaborated in detail; Secondly, the Adam optimization algorithm is adopted to conduct systematic training and parameter optimization for the model; Ultimately, a complete deep learning model for fault identification was constructed. To verify the performance of the model, simulation experiments were carried out. The results show that the proposed method significantly outperforms the comparison models (such as SVM, BPNN, DBN, RNN) in terms of accuracy, reaching up to 91.6 % at the highest. Furthermore, the newly added class imbalance robustness test indicates that the model still maintains stable performance when the sample distribution is uneven, while the Ablation Study quantifies the positive contribution of the Adam algorithm to the convergence speed and accuracy of the model. The model proposed in this paper can effectively meet the requirements of fault identification in distribution networks and provide reliable technical support for the safe operation of distribution networks.

title: Design, Development, and Evaluation of an Intelligent Generation System for Contextualized Primary School Science Test Questions
authors: Xuan Yang, Yajie Wen, Xuebo Zhang, Zehui Zhan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Educational Technology Management (ICETM)
abstract: To solve the problems of low efficiency and uneven quality in the traditional manual creation of context-based science questions for primary schools, this study designed and validated an intelligent generation system named “SciQ-Gen”. The system uses a large language model as its core. It introduces an innovative “Dual-channel Retrieval-Augmented Generation” framework to improve both the engagement of the scenarios and the scientific accuracy. The system also uses a “Generate-Evaluate-Revise” iterative process for quality control. Furthermore, this research developed a quality evaluation framework for test questions that includes four dimensions: “scenario quality”, “question quality”, “knowledge and skill depth”, and “inquiry level”. In a comparative experiment, three experts used this framework to anonymously score questions from three sources: those generated by SciQ-Gen, those from regional public examinations, and those from international assessments such as PISA and TIMSS. The research confirmed that questions generated by SciQ-Gen performed exceptionally well in “depth of knowledge and skills” and “inquiry level”. However, their performance in “scenario quality” and “question quality” was slightly weaker than the questions from the international assessments. The study also shows that its evaluation framework provides a scientific and practical standard for assessing context-based test questions.

title: Implementing Precise Translation between English and Other Languages Using Efficient Algorithms
authors: Yali Li, Ying Zhan, Leqi Zhang
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Computer Communication, Networks and Information Science (CCNIS)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid algorithmic framework for precise neural machine translation between English and other languages, emphasizing resilience to adversarial attacks and lowresource challenges. The proposed system integrates stochastic noise injection into the embedding space and a dual-stage gradient masking mechanism within a modular neural architecture. Randomized smoothing is applied to regulate the model's decision boundaries, while dynamic noise scheduling optimizes the tradeoff between translation quality and robustness during inference. Experiments conducted on the IWSLT2017 multilingual translation benchmark, using a Transformer backbone, demonstrate that the defended model maintains 99 % of cleandata BLEU performance even under strong FGSM and PGD adversarial perturbations. Certified robustness guaranties are verified by mathematically deriving the stability of the translation output within a defined perturbation radius. To ensure real-time feasibility, the total computational overhead is kept below a standard baseline. Comprehensive ablation studies show that the defense mechanism works in multiple languages and threat environments. These results establish that embedding architectural defenses directly into the translation pipeline delivers both practical security and high translation fidelity, providing a robust solution for secure multilingual neural machine translation.

title: QuEST: Quantization-conditioned Efficient Stealthy Trojan
authors: Liming Lu, Shuchao Pang, Jiakai Wang, Xiang Gu, Yunhuai Liu, Xianglong Liu, Yongbin Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Quantization-conditioned backdoor attacks, which exploit model quantization states to trigger malicious behavior, pose a hidden threat to deep learning security. However, current studies ignore the feasibility of attacks, i.e., detection visibility, and computational overhead, leading to significant constraints on their practical deployment in real-world adversarial scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose Quantization-conditioned Efficient Stealthy Trojan (QuEST), a novel framework that enhances both the stealth and efficiency of backdoor attacks under quantization constraints. For enhancing stealthiness, we design a stealth-optimized training scheme that benefits from the parametric backdoor injection and trigger scaling augmentation to maintain the consistency of model behavior during attack. In this way, the defender will fail to capture the suspicious behavior differences for detection due to the made efforts in both model-side and data-side. To improve efficiency, we introduce information-guided parameter sharing, which utilizes parameter redundancy analysis and Fisher divergence metrics to identify a minimal amount of quantization-preserved parameters for back-door injection. These parameters are strategically shared between the malicious and benign models, enabling concurrent training and substantially reducing overall training time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QuEST maintains competitive attack success rates while improving stealth performance by 18.75% and reducing computational costs by 26.66% on average compared to state-of-the-art methods, highlighting QuEST’s potential for more practical adversarial deployments in real-world scenarios. Our code is available here.

title: Generative Diffusion Models for Wireless Networks: Fundamental, Architecture, and State-of-the-Art
authors: Dayu Fan, Rui Meng, Xiaodong Xu, Yiming Liu, Guoshun Nan, Chenyuan Feng, Shujun Han, Song Gao, Bingxuan Xu, Dusit Niyato, Tony Q. S. Quek, Ping Zhang
abstract: With the rapid development of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GAI) technology, Generative Diffusion Models (GDMs) have shown significant empowerment potential in the field of wireless networks due to advantages, such as noise resistance, training stability, controllability, and multimodal generation. Although there have been multiple studies focusing on GDMs for wireless networks, there is still a lack of comprehensive reviews on their technological evolution. Motivated by this, we systematically explore the application of GDMs in wireless networks. Firstly, we identify the core challenges of wireless networks and argue why GDMs are uniquely suited to address them. We then introduce the mathematical principles of GDMs and representative models. Furthermore, we organize our comprehensive review through a structured taxonomy that categorizes GDM-based schemes into the sensing, transmission, and Applications, complemented by a security plane. For each representative scheme, we analyze its innovative points, the role of GDMs, strengths, and weaknesses. Ultimately, we extract key challenges and provide potential solutions, with the aim of providing directional guidance for future research in this field.
